facts: Legendary founder-figure of the early Islamic-era royal line.
Tradition describes him as arriving from Ceylon and establishing rule in Malé.
Traditionally described as arriving from Ceylon and establishing the first Islamic-era royal line in Malé.
people.no: The legendary ruler of the Lion Race from Ceylon.

known_as.name: Koimala Kalō
known_as.note: Traditional Dhivehi name-form for the legendary founder.

known_as.name: Theemuge Maha Kalaminja
known_as.note: Formal royal style attested in historical king-lists.

people.bio:
Koimala, the legendary first sovereign of the Maldivian royal succession, occupies a liminal space between myth and history. The chronicle traditions describe him as a prince of the Lunar (Soma) dynasty who arrived from Ceylon and unified the scattered island communities under a single royal authority, establishing his capital at Malikaddudhemedhu around 1117 CE. His full Sanskritic titulature, Svasti Sri Somavamsa Adipati Sri Theemuge Maha Parama Aditya Maha Radun, places him firmly within the Indo-Aryan royal naming conventions of the pre-Islamic Indian Ocean world, claiming descent from the ancient Lunar lineage of Indian kingship.

The historical kernel within the Koimala legend likely reflects a genuine political transition in the early twelfth century. The Chola Empire had dominated the Maldives and much of the Indian Ocean since the conquests of Rajaraja Chola I around 1010 CE. Koimala is credited with defeating or displacing the remnants of Chola authority in the northern atolls, an achievement that would have required both military capability and the political legitimacy conferred by a royal pedigree. His regnal name Maanaabarana, from the Sanskrit maha-abharana meaning "Great Ornament," is entirely consistent with the Buddhist-Sinhalese cultural matrix of the pre-Islamic Maldives.

RoyalArk genealogies identify Koimala's father as Svasti Sri Buwana Ananda Maha Radun, a pre-Islamic Lunar king, and note that his grandfather was Svasti Sri Loka Abaruna Maha Radun. These names, though impossible to verify independently, suggest a claimed patrilineage of at least three generations of Lunar dynasty rulers before Koimala's accession. More significantly for the dynastic succession, Koimala's sister Henevi Ma'ava'a Kilege became the mother of Dhovemi, the second sovereign, establishing the matrilineal link that would characterize early Maldivian succession patterns.

Koimala is recorded as having died before July 26, 1142. He left no recorded direct heirs; the throne passed instead to his nephew Dhovemi through the matrilineal connection. The Raadhavalhi chronicle gives his full regnal style as Mahaabarana Mahaaradhun, a variant of the Maanaabarana form preserved in the standard king-lists. Whatever the historical reality behind the founding legend, Koimala's establishment of the Theemuge royal house initiated a dynastic tradition that would persist, through conversions, depositions, and dynastic changes, for over eight centuries.

The tradition of Koimala's Ceylonese origin reflects the deep cultural and linguistic connections between the Maldives and Sri Lanka. The Dhivehi language itself descends from the same Insular Indo-Aryan branch as Sinhalese, and the material culture of the pre-Islamic Maldives, including Buddhist stupas, inscriptions, and architectural forms documented by H.C.P. Bell in the early twentieth century, confirms extensive Sinhalese influence. Whether Koimala was literally a Sinhalese prince or whether the legend projects back onto a founding figure the cultural affinities that undeniably existed, his story encapsulates the pre-Islamic identity of the Maldivian polity.

people.no: Reigned as Buddhist until 1153 conversion to Islam. Son of Koimala’s sister.
facts: Traditionally remembered as Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu in later chronicles and lists.
After conversion to Islam in 1153 CE, adopted the Arabic title Sultan Muhammad al-Adil. Both Ibn Battuta and the Tarikh tradition agree on this post-conversion title.

known_as.name: Dhovemi Kalaminjaa
known_as.note: Used in Dhivehi historical naming forms.

known_as.name: Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu
known_as.note: Honorific epithet attached to Prince/Sultan Dhovemi in historical lists.

known_as.name: Sultan Muhammad al-Adil
known_as.note: Post-conversion Arabic title meaning "The Just". Earliest Arabic sovereign title in Maldivian history, adopted after the 1153 CE conversion to Islam.

people.bio:
Dhovemi, the second sovereign of the Maldives, presided over the single most consequential event in the archipelago's history: the conversion of the islands from Buddhism to Islam in 1153 CE. His full Sanskritic regnal style, Svasti Sri Sandanayaka Tribhuwana Aditya Maha Radun (Bavanaadheeththa), marks him as the last Maldivian ruler to bear a purely Indic title before the adoption of Arabic nomenclature. After conversion, he took the Islamic name Sultan Muhammad al-Adil, meaning "The Just," becoming the first Muslim monarch of the Maldives.

Dhovemi was the son of Henevi Ma'ava'a Kilege, the sister of the founding sovereign Koimala, making him Koimala's nephew. His father's personal name is not recorded in the surviving chronicles, reflecting the strongly matrilineal character of early Maldivian succession patterns. He succeeded his uncle before July 26, 1142, and his pre-conversion reign of approximately eleven years saw the continuation of the Buddhist-Sinhalese political order that had characterized the Maldives for centuries.

The conversion to Islam on July 7, 1153 is attributed in competing traditions to different figures. The Moroccan traveler Ibn Battuta, who visited the Maldives nearly two centuries later, credited Abu'l-Baraka'at the Berber, a North African holy man who supposedly expelled a sea-demon (rannamaari) that terrorized the islanders. The local Tarikh tradition, however, credits Abul Rikab Yusuf Shams ud-din with the conversion. Whatever the historical mechanism, the shift from Buddhism to Islam was rapid and thoroughgoing, transforming not only the religious identity but also the political vocabulary, legal framework, and cultural orientation of the entire archipelago.

After a reign of approximately twenty-five years, Dhovemi did not die in the Maldives but departed on pilgrimage to Mecca before October 28, 1166, never to return. This departure for the holy cities, whether voluntary abdication or political exile dressed in pious garb, established a pattern that would recur in Maldivian royal history: sultans departing the islands and never resuming their thrones. His post-conversion Arabic title Sultan Muhammad al-Adil ibn Abdullah reflects the standard Islamic convention of assigning "ibn Abdullah" (son of a servant of God) to converts whose fathers were not Muslim, a practice that would mark the first generation of Maldivian Islamic rulers.

Dhovemi's legacy is foundational. Every subsequent Maldivian sovereign would rule as a Muslim monarch, and the Islamic identity he inaugurated has persisted unbroken to the present day. The traditional chronicle accounts honor him with the epithet Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu, and he is remembered as the sovereign who brought the light of Islam to the scattered coral islands of the Indian Ocean.

people.no: Maternal cousin of #2.
people.bio:
Muthey, the third sovereign, bore the regnal name Bavana Abarana and the full Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Buwana Parama Maha Radun (Buvanaabarana). He was the son of Mulee Ma'ava'a Kilege, following the early Maldivian convention of identifying rulers through their mothers. The relationship between Muthey and his predecessor Dhovemi is described variously as fraternal or cousinly; RoyalArk states he "succeeded brother" Dhovemi, while the project data describes him as a maternal cousin. This ambiguity likely reflects the loose kinship terminology of the early chronicles.

Muthey's accession before October 28, 1166, coincided with Dhovemi's departure for Mecca. His coronation is recorded before July 10, 1176. He died before April 4, 1185, having reigned approximately nineteen years during the earliest decades of Islamic rule. His reign represents a period of consolidation after the momentous conversion of 1153, though the chronicles preserve few specific details of his governance.

people.no: Son of Rekeihiriya Maava Kilege.
people.bio:
Ali, the fourth sovereign, bore the regnal name Dhammaru Naaja and the full Sanskritic titulature Svasti Sri Sarvalaksha Adipati Sri Dharmananda Maha Radun. He was the son of Reki Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage, a matrilineal identification consistent with the early Lunar dynasty practice. His accession before April 4, 1185, followed the death of Muthey.

Ali's reign lasted approximately eight years, ending with his death before January 7, 1193. The Sanskritic elements of his regnal style, particularly the compound Dharmananda ("Bliss of Righteousness"), demonstrate the continuing vitality of the pre-Islamic naming tradition four decades after the conversion. Ali was succeeded by Dhinei (P5), the first of the four sons of Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage who would reign in unbroken succession.

people.no: Son of Fathahiriya Maava Kilege.
people.bio:
Dhinei, the fifth sovereign of the Maldives, reigned from approximately 1193 to 1199 and is remembered primarily for his role in the violent suppression of Buddhism in the southern atolls. His Sanskritic regnal style, Srimant Gadannaditya Maha Radun (Fennaadheeththa), and his birth name Prince Dinei Kalaminja reflect the pre-Islamic naming conventions that persisted among the Lunar dynasty rulers long after the conversion to Islam in 1153.

Dhinei was the son of Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P216), a woman of evident political importance who bore four sons who all became sultans: Dhinei himself, Dhilhel (P6), Wadi (P7), and Valla Dio (P8). This remarkable maternal fertility created a stable succession that kept the throne within a single sibling group for over sixty years.

The most historically significant event of Dhinei's reign was his destruction of the Buddhist monastery on Isdhoo (Laamu Atoll) in 1195, approximately forty years after the national conversion to Islam. He ordered forced conversions, executed monks who resisted, and in 1197 constructed a mosque on the site of the former monastery. Archaeological evidence from the Isdhoo region, including the famous Isdhoo Loamaafaanu copper plate inscriptions, confirms the existence of significant Buddhist establishments in the southern atolls. Dhinei's violent iconoclasm suggests that the conversion to Islam was not as swift or comprehensive as the official narrative implies, and that Buddhist communities persisted in the outer atolls for decades after the royal court embraced Islam.

Dhinei died before October 23, 1199, and was succeeded by his younger brother Dhilhel (P6).

people.no: Younger brother of #5.
people.bio:
Dhilhel, the sixth sovereign, bore the regnal name Dhagathaa Abarana and the Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Dagata Abaruna Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Dihai Kalaminja. He was the second of the four sons of Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P216) to rule, succeeding his elder brother Dhinei (P5) before October 23, 1199.

Dhilhel reigned for approximately fifteen years, dying before May 13, 1214. The stability implied by the fraternal succession from Dhinei to Dhilhel, and subsequently to Wadi (P7) and Valla Dio (P8), suggests a remarkably cohesive royal family in this period. The four brothers' combined reigns covered over sixty years, from 1193 to 1258, constituting the longest period of uninterrupted succession within a single sibling group in Maldivian history.

people.no: Younger brother of #6.
people.bio:
Wadi, the seventh sovereign, bore the regnal name Dhagathaa Suvara and the style Svasti Sri Dagata Subarna Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Wadi Kalaminja, and he was the third son of Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P216). He succeeded his brother Dhilhel (P6) before May 13, 1214, and reigned until his death before October 7, 1233.

Wadi's significance extends beyond his own reign through his son, Prince Uddu Kalaminja, who later ruled as Audha (P13). Through Audha, Wadi became the ancestor of the entire later Lunar dynasty, including Yoosuf (P15), Salis (P16), Omar Veeru (P18), Khadijah (P20), and Raadhaafathi (P25). The genealogical weight of this lineage makes Wadi the most consequential of the four brothers in dynastic terms, even though his nineteen-year reign left fewer specific historical traces than those of his descendants.

people.no: Younger brother of #7.
people.bio:
Valla Dio, the eighth sovereign, bore the regnal name Raa-Araa Desyara and the full Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Radha Subarna Rana-ara Desvaru Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Valladio Kalaminja, and he was the youngest of the four sons of Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P216). He succeeded his brother Wadi (P7) before October 7, 1233.

Valla Dio's reign of approximately twenty-five years, ending with his death before January 8, 1258, was the longest of the four brothers. He was the last of this remarkable fraternal sequence to hold the throne. After his death, the succession passed to a different branch of the Lunar dynasty through Hudhei (P9), whose mother was a different woman named Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P205). The transition from Valla Dio's line marks the end of the Fahi Hiriya branch's direct hold on power.

people.no: Son of Hiriya Maava Kilege.
people.bio:
Hudhei, the ninth sovereign, bore the regnal name Raadha Suvara and the Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Radha Subarna Vira Abaruna Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Hudai Kalaminja. He was the son of Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P205), a different woman from the Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage who mothered the previous four sovereigns.

Hudhei's accession before January 8, 1258, marked a shift in the succession away from the direct fraternal line of P5 through P8. His reign of approximately six years, ending before October 24, 1264, was modest in duration. The transition from the Fahi Hiriya branch to Hudhei's line reflects the broader pattern of Lunar dynasty succession, in which the throne moved between different matrilineal branches as direct male lines were exhausted.

people.no: Son of Volumidi Bodu Kilege.
people.bio:
Aima, the tenth sovereign, bore the regnal name Loka Suvara and the Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Loka Subarna Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Iyama Kalaminja. He was the son of Volumidi Bodu Kilege of Fehendu, and his mother was Hirati Kabadi Kilage (P224).

Aima's reign was brief, lasting from before October 24, 1264, to his deposition or death before October 2, 1266, a period of approximately two years. The identification of both his parents in the RoyalArk genealogies is unusual for this early period, when most sovereigns are identified solely through their mothers. His father's connection to Fehendu, an atoll island, suggests that the Lunar dynasty's reach extended well beyond Male even in the thirteenth century.

people.no: Son of Vengihi Kalo and Aidage Maava Kilege.
people.bio:
Hali, the eleventh sovereign, bore the regnal name Singaa Abarana and the Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Sanya Abaruna Maha Radun (Sultan Ali II). His birth name was Prince Hili Kalaminja. He was the son of Vengihi Kalo (P204) and Aidage Maava Kilege (P203), making him one of the few early sovereigns for whom both parents are securely identified.

Hali's reign lasted approximately one year and six months, from before October 2, 1266, to his deposition or death before September 10, 1268. He shared his mother with his successor Keimi (P12), continuing the pattern of matrilineal succession that characterized the mid-thirteenth-century Lunar dynasty. The relatively rapid succession of short-reigning sovereigns in this period, from Hudhei through Keimi, suggests political instability as competing branches of the Lunar house vied for the throne.

people.no: Son of Aidage Maava Kilege.
people.bio:
Keimi, the twelfth sovereign, bore the regnal name Madheenee Suvara and the Sanskritic style Svasti Sri Madini Subarna Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Keimi Kalaminja. He was the son of Aidage Maava Kilege (P203), sharing his mother with his predecessor Hali (P11), though his father was not the same as Hali's father Vengihi Kalo (P204).

Keimi's reign was the shortest of the mid-Lunar period, lasting from before September 10, 1268, to his deposition or death before August 31, 1269, a span of less than a year. The fact that Keimi and Hali shared a mother but had different fathers exemplifies the matrilineal emphasis in early Maldivian succession: it was the mother's royal blood, not the father's, that conferred legitimacy. After Keimi, the throne returned to the line of Wadi (P7) through his son Audha (P13).

people.no: Son of #7.
people.bio:
Audha, the thirteenth sovereign, bore the regnal name Areedha Suvara and the Arabic-Sanskritic title Sultan Muhammad Svasti Sri Ra'aradha Subarna Maha Radun. His birth name was Prince Uddu Kalaminja. He was the son of Wadi (P7), the seventh sovereign, through whom he traced his descent from the Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage branch of the Lunar dynasty.

Audha's accession before August 31, 1269, brought the throne back to the main Lunar patriline after the brief reigns of Hudhei, Aima, Hali, and Keimi. His nine-year reign, ending before May 25, 1278, provided relative stability. His Arabic name Sultan Muhammad, alongside his Sanskritic regnal style, reflects the deepening Islamization of the Maldivian court in the late thirteenth century. Audha fathered two sons who both became sultans: Hali, known as Ali III (P14), and Yoosuf (P15), through whom the entire later Lunar dynasty descended.

people.no: Son of #13.
people.bio:
Hali, the fourteenth sovereign and also known as Ali III, bore the same regnal name as his father: Areedha Suvara. His Arabic title was Sultan Ali III ibnu Sultan Muhammad, explicitly invoking his paternal lineage from Audha. He succeeded his father before May 25, 1278.

Hali's ten-year reign ended with his death before February 5, 1288. The most significant genealogical fact recorded about him is that he died without issue. The absence of a direct heir meant that the throne passed to his younger brother Yoosuf (P15), whose descendants would constitute the entire later Lunar dynasty. Had Hali produced sons, the subsequent history of the dynasty might have unfolded very differently.

people.no: Younger brother of #14.
people.bio:
Yoosuf, the fifteenth sovereign, reigned from approximately 1288 to 1294 and served as a crucial genealogical pivot in the later Lunar dynasty. His Sanskritic regnal name Bavanaadheeththa and his Arabic title Sultan Yusuf ibn Sultan Muhammad link him to the succession of mid-Lunar rulers who presided over a stable, if unremarkable, period in Maldivian history.

Yoosuf was the younger brother of Hali (P14), and both were sons of Audha (P13), who was himself the son of Wadi (P7). When Hali died without issue before February 5, 1288, the throne passed laterally to Yoosuf, maintaining the succession within the same branch of the Lunar house. Yoosuf died before November 21, 1294.

Yoosuf's primary historical significance lies in his two sons: Salis (P16) and Davud (P17), who succeeded each other on the throne. More importantly, Salis fathered Omar Veeru (P18), the longest-reigning Lunar sultan, whose children Khadijah (P20) and Ahmad Shihab al-Din (P19) would dominate the dramatic final decades of the Lunar dynasty. Through this lineage, Yoosuf stands as the common ancestor of the entire late-Lunar royal house.

people.no: Son of #15.
people.bio:
Salis, the sixteenth sovereign, bore the regnal name Meesuvvara and the full Arabic title Sultan Abul Fath Salah ud-din Salis al-Banjali. He was the elder son of Yoosuf (P15) and the father of Omar Veeru (P18), placing him at a critical juncture in the Lunar dynasty's genealogy.

The "al-Banjali" element in his Arabic titulature is significant: it connects to Ibn Battuta's later reference to "Sultan Salahuddin Salih al-Bangali," suggesting a claimed or attributed Bengali origin for this branch of the Lunar dynasty. Whether this Bengali connection was real or legendary remains debated, but it places the mid-Lunar dynasty within the broader Indian Ocean world of Bengali, Sinhalese, and Arab cultural exchange.

Salis reigned from approximately 1293 to 1302, dying before August 26 of that year. His principal legacy was his son Omar Veeru (P18), whose thirty-four-year reign would be the Lunar dynasty's golden age. The RoyalArk genealogies confirm the parent-child relationship between Salis and Omar, resolving the ambiguity in some sources that described them as half-brothers.

people.no: Son of #15.
people.bio:
Davud, the seventeenth sovereign, bore the regnal name Sundhura Bavana and the full Arabic-Sanskritic title Sultan Daud ibnu Sultan Yusuf Svasti Sri Singha Buwana Maha Radun. He was the younger son of Yoosuf (P15) and the brother of Salis (P16).

Davud's reign from approximately 1302 to 1307 ended with his death before July 3, 1307. He left only one unnamed daughter and no legitimate male heirs, a circumstance that determined the succession. The throne passed not to his daughter but to his nephew Omar Veeru (P18), son of Salis. Ibn Battuta later noted Davud as a grandson of "Sultan Ahmad Shanuraza," an identification that remains difficult to reconcile with the standard genealogy. Davud's failure to produce male heirs brought the Salis-Davud fraternal pair to an end and concentrated the Lunar dynasty's future entirely in the line of Omar Veeru.

people.no: Son of #16 (Salis). RoyalArk confirms parentage. Full Arabic title: Sultan Abul Fath Jalal ud-dunya wa ud-din Umar Vira.
facts: Son of Salis (P16), resolving the longstanding ambiguity about whether P18 was half-brother or son of P16.
Had four known children: Ahmad Shihab ud-din (P19), Khadijah (P20), Mariam (unmodeled), and Fatima/Raadhaafathi (P25).
Second wife was daughter of Vizier Jamil ud-din Assinjari.
Longest reign of the Lunar dynasty: 34 years (1307-1341).

known_as.name: Sultan Abul Fath Jalal ud-dunya wa ud-din Umar Vira
known_as.note: Complete Arabic titulature from RoyalArk.

known_as.name: Svasti Sri Loka Abaruna Maha Radun
known_as.note: Full Sanskritic regnal style.

people.bio:
Omar Veeru, bearing the magnificent Arabic titulature Sultan Abul Fath Jalal ud-dunya wa ud-din Umar Vira, was the eighteenth sovereign of the Maldives and the longest-reigning monarch of the Lunar dynasty, holding the throne for approximately thirty-four years (1307-1341). His Sanskritic regnal style Svasti Sri Loka Abaruna Maha Radun (Loka Abarana) preserved the pre-Islamic naming conventions alongside his Arabic titles, embodying the cultural hybridity of medieval Maldivian kingship.

Omar Veeru was the son of Salis (P16), resolving a longstanding ambiguity in the genealogical record about whether he was the half-brother or son of his predecessor. His accession before July 3, 1307 came after the death of his uncle Davud (P17), who had left no legitimate male heirs. Omar's long and stable reign contrasted sharply with the rapid succession of short-lived rulers in the preceding decades of the Lunar dynasty.

Omar married twice. His first wife, whose name is not recorded, bore his son Ahmad Shihab ud-din (P19) and daughter Khadijah (P20), who would become the most famous female ruler in Maldivian history. His second wife was the daughter of Vizier Jamil ud-din Assinjari, and she may have been the mother of his other daughters: Mariam and Fatima, the latter also known as Raadhaafathi (P25). Through his children, Omar Veeru's bloodline dominated the remaining decades of Lunar dynasty rule.

Omar Veeru died before June 17, 1341, leaving behind a kingdom that was prosperous and relatively stable by medieval Indian Ocean standards. His reign coincided with the era of expanding Indian Ocean trade networks, and the Maldives occupied a strategic position along the maritime routes connecting Arabia, India, and Southeast Asia. The four children who survived him would produce the dramatic final chapter of the Lunar dynasty, marked by Khadijah's three accessions, the assassinations of two sultans, and the eventual displacement of the Lunar house by the Hilaaly dynasty in 1388.

people.no: Deposed by elder sister Khadijah. Son of #18.
facts: Ibn Battuta visited the Maldives during or shortly after Ahmad Shihab al-Din’s deposition by his sister Khadijah (P20) in 1347.

known_as.name: Ahmad Shihab al-Din
known_as.note: Arabic name form attested by Ibn Battuta who was present during or shortly after his reign.

people.bio:
Ahmad Shihab al-Din, the nineteenth sovereign of the Maldives, is one of the most vividly documented figures of the early period thanks to the accounts of Ibn Battuta, who visited the archipelago during or shortly after his reign. Born around 1326, he was the son of Omar Veeru (P18) and the elder brother of the formidable Sultana Khadijah (P20). His Arabic laqab Shihab al-Din, meaning "Star of the Faith," followed the conventions of Arabic royal nomenclature that had become standard since the conversion.

Ahmad succeeded his father before June 17, 1341, but his reign was troubled from the outset. He governed under the regency of the powerful Vizier Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Hadhrami, who effectively controlled the machinery of state. The young sultan's personal life was equally complicated: he was briefly married to a daughter of Sultan Muhammad Jamil ud-din, though the marriage was reportedly never consummated, and later married a lady from Mulaku Atoll. Most remarkably, Ahmad was briefly married to Ibn Battuta himself in March 1343, a union that lasted barely a year before divorce.

Ahmad's reign ended violently when his elder sister Khadijah deposed him in 1347, seizing the throne for herself in what amounted to a palace coup facilitated by the vizier class. The deposed sultan was subsequently assassinated at Haladummati before April 2, 1348. His short and unhappy reign illustrates the precarious nature of royal authority in fourteenth-century Maldives, where the power of viziers and the ambitions of royal women could easily overwhelm a young and politically weak sovereign.

facts: One of the most prominent women rulers of the Maldives.
Held the throne in three separate accessions.
Most prominent female ruler in Maldivian history, holding the throne across three separate accessions (1347-1363, 1364-1374, 1376-1380).
Deposed her brother Ahmed Shihabuddine (P19) to seize power in 1347.
Ibn Battuta visited during her reign (c. 1343-1345) and served as chief qadi (judge); he noted that orders were issued in her name only and written on palm leaves.

Married and is said to have arranged the assassination of at least two husbands: Wazir Jamal al-Din/Mohamed el-Jameel (P21, possibly Egyptian) and Wazir Abdallah ibn Muhammad al-Hadhrami (P23, from Hadhramaut, Yemen).
Ibn Battuta recorded that her grandfather was "Sultan Salahuddin Salih al-Bangali" — a contested Bengali connection not confirmed by the Tarikh chronicle tradition.
people.no: Three accessions. Daughter of #18. Assassinated husbands #21 and #23.

known_as.name: Sultana Khadijah
known_as.note: Arabic formal title as ruling queen, attested by Ibn Battuta who visited during her first reign.

known_as.name: Raadha Abarana
known_as.note: Regnal epithet attested in king-lists.

known_as.name: Al-Sultana Khadijah
known_as.note: Arabic sovereign title form used by Ibn Battuta. He described orders issued in her name written on palm leaves.

known_as.name: Al-Sultana Khadeejah Sri Raadha Abaarana Mahaa Rehendhi
known_as.note: Full Dhivehi-Arabic compound style combining Sultana title with Dhivehi regnal epithets.

people.bio:
Khadijah, the most prominent female ruler in Maldivian history, held the throne across three separate accessions spanning over three decades (1347-1363, 1364-1374, 1376-1380), an extraordinary record of political survival in a society where female sovereignty was deeply contested. Her full regnal style, Al-Sultana Khadeejah Sri Raadha Abaarana Mahaa Rehendhi, combined the Arabic sovereign title with the traditional Dhivehi regnal epithets, and she bore the additional epithet "Sultana of Land and Sea." Before her accession she was known by the court title Kamba'adi Kilege.

Khadijah was the daughter of Omar Veeru (P18), the longest-reigning Lunar dynasty sultan, and likely the daughter of his second wife, a daughter of Vizier Jamil ud-din Assinjari. She seized the throne from her brother Ahmad Shihab al-Din (P19) in 1347, reportedly with the assistance of the vizier class who preferred a pliant queen to an independent young sultan. The deposed Ahmad was subsequently assassinated at Haladummati, and Khadijah began a reign characterized by both political ruthlessness and administrative competence.

Ibn Battuta visited the Maldives during her reign, serving as the chief qadi (judge) of the islands between approximately 1343 and 1345. His account provides the most detailed contemporary portrait of any medieval Maldivian ruler. He noted that governmental orders were issued in Khadijah's name alone, written on palm leaves in the Maldivian script, and that she personally presided over court audiences. Famously, Ibn Battuta attempted to impose stricter Islamic dress codes on the women of the islands and was rebuffed, an episode that suggests both the limits of clerical authority and the independence of Maldivian women under Khadijah's rule.

Khadijah married and is said to have arranged the assassination of two husband-sultans: Wazir Jamal al-Din (P21), who died before January 7, 1345, and Wazir Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Hadhrami (P23), who was murdered in bed before May 10, 1377. She bore one posthumous child by Jameel. These violent eliminations of consort-sultans demonstrate that Khadijah regarded the throne as fundamentally hers, not to be shared with ambitious husbands who might try to rule in their own right.

Khadijah's three separate reigns were punctuated by brief intervals when male claimants temporarily displaced her, but she repeatedly recovered the throne through a combination of political alliance, popular support, and the absence of viable male alternatives within the Lunar royal house. She died before April 8, 1380, and was succeeded by her half-sister Raadhaafathi (P25), the last Lunar dynasty queen. Ibn Battuta's contested claim that her grandfather was "Sultan Salahuddin Salih al-Bangali" has generated scholarly debate about a possible Bengali connection in the Lunar line, but this attribution is not confirmed by the Tarikh chronicle tradition.

Khadijah's historical significance extends beyond the Maldives. She represents one of the few examples of sustained female Islamic sovereignty in the medieval world, and her ability to maintain power across three decades in a male-dominated religious and political environment speaks to the persistence of matrilineal traditions in Maldivian society that predated the Islamic conversion.

people.no: Assassinated by wife Khadijah (#20).
facts: Ibn Battuta identifies him as the wazir who helped Khadijah seize power from her brother Ahmad Shihab al-Din (P19).
The name "el-Jameel" in the project data corresponds to Arabic "Jamal al-Din" (Beauty of the Faith).

known_as.name: Wazir Jamal al-Din
known_as.note: Arabic name form from Ibn Battuta. Jamal/Jameel are the same Arabic root (j-m-l). Served as wazir and first husband of Khadijah (P20).

known_as.name: Jamal al-Din al-Misri
known_as.note: Extended form with "al-Misri" (the Egyptian) epithet sometimes attached in secondary accounts. Geographic origin uncertain.

people.bio:
Mohamed el-Jameel, known in Arabic as Sultan Muhammad Jamil ud-din, was the twenty-first sovereign of the Maldives and the first husband of the formidable Sultana Khadijah (P20). His full titulature, Svasti Sri Buwana Suriya Maha Radun, combined the traditional Sanskritic regnal style with his Arabic name, reflecting the dual naming conventions of fourteenth-century Maldivian royalty. Ibn Battuta identifies him as the wazir (chief minister) who helped Khadijah seize power from her brother Ahmad Shihab al-Din (P19) in 1347.

Jameel's origins are obscure. Some secondary accounts attach the epithet "al-Misri" (the Egyptian), suggesting a possible North African provenance, though this geographic attribution remains uncertain. What is clear is that he served as Grand Vizier before his elevation to the sultanate through marriage to the ruling queen. He had children from a previous marriage, including a son Muhammad Ha'adiyari Khatib and a daughter who later married Ibn Battuta himself.

Jameel's reign was brief and ended in his death before January 7, 1345. The Maldivian chronicle tradition, corroborated by Ibn Battuta's account, holds that Khadijah arranged his assassination, establishing a grim pattern that would repeat with her second husband Abdullah (P23). His Arabic name Jamil ud-din, meaning "Beauty of the Faith," rendered in the project data as "el-Jameel," preserves the same Arabic root j-m-l that appears across both forms.

people.no: Assassinated by wife Khadijah (#20).
facts: Ibn Battuta identifies him as the second husband and wazir of Khadijah (P20), of Hadhrami (Yemeni) origin.
The Hadhramaut connection is significant: Hadhrami merchants and scholars were major vectors of Islamic influence across the Indian Ocean.

known_as.name: Abdallah ibn Muhammad al-Hadhrami
known_as.note: Full Arabic name from Ibn Battuta. The "al-Hadhrami" epithet indicates Hadhramaut (Yemen) origin.

people.bio:
Abdullah ibn Muhammad al-Hadhrami, the twenty-third sovereign of the Maldives, was Khadijah's second husband-sultan and, like his predecessor Jameel (P21), met his end at her hands. His nisba "al-Hadhrami" indicates origins in the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, connecting him to the networks of Hadhrami merchants and scholars who served as major vectors of Islamic influence across the Indian Ocean from East Africa to Southeast Asia.

Abdullah was the son of Muhammad al-Hadhrami, the powerful vizier who had served as regent during the reign of Ahmad Shihab al-Din (P19). Before his elevation to the sultanate, Abdullah had a complex marital history: he had married a daughter of Sultan Muhammad Jamil ud-din (P21), been divorced, and then married the widow of Sultan Omar Veeru (P18) around 1342. His marriage to Sultana Khadijah (P20) in 1345 brought him to the throne as her consort-sultan.

Abdullah reigned from before June 13, 1374 until his murder before May 10, 1377. The Maldivian chronicles record that Khadijah had him killed "in bed," a detail that underscores the intimate and violent nature of court politics in the fourteenth-century sultanate. He left one daughter, whose name is not preserved in the sources. His Hadhramaut connection is historically significant beyond his personal fate, representing the broader pattern of Arab immigration that shaped the religious and cultural character of the post-conversion Maldives.

facts: Late-Lunar sovereign in the succession crisis period.
Half-sister of Khadijah, continuing the same royal house.
people.no: Half-sister of Khadijah (#20); daughter of #18.
people.bio:
Raadhaafathi, the twenty-fifth sovereign, bore the full title Malika Rehendi Fatima Svasti Sri Somu Abaruna Maha Rehendi. Her pre-accession title was Ra'adafathi Kamba'adi Kilege. She was a daughter of Omar Veeru (P18) and the half-sister of Khadijah (P20), making her one of two daughters of Omar to hold the throne.

Radhaafathi came to power before April 8, 1380, succeeding her half-sister Khadijah. Her brief reign lasted less than a year: she abdicated before March 28, 1381, in favor of her husband, Mohamed of Maakurathu (P26). This voluntary abdication stands in contrast to the forced depositions that characterized many Maldivian successions. She bore one daughter, Dhaain (P27), who would become the third and last of the female sultanas in the late Lunar succession.

Radhaafathi's reign was the second of the three female reigns in the late fourteenth-century Maldives. While her half-sister Khadijah had demonstrated that a woman could rule effectively for decades, Raadhaafathi's quick abdication in favor of her husband suggests either political pressure or personal preference for a different arrangement of power. She died around 1385.

people.no: Husband of #25.
people.bio:
Mohamed of Maakurathu, the twenty-sixth sovereign, bore the regnal name Sundhura Abarana and the title Sultan Muhammad Svasti Sri Suddha Abaruna Maha Radhun. His father was Kaulanna Kilage of Ma'akurati in South Ma'alosmadulu Atoll, making him an outsider to the Lunar royal house who gained the throne through marriage.

Mohamed came to power through the abdication of his wife Raadhaafathi (P25) before March 28, 1381. This marital path to sovereignty, while unusual, was not unprecedented in a polity where female rulers had established the precedent that the throne could pass through women. Mohamed and Raadhaafathi had one daughter, Dhaain (P27), through whom the Lunar dynasty's female line would make its final appearance on the throne. Mohamed died before February 12, 1385, having reigned approximately four years.

facts: Succeeded in a turbulent late-Lunar succession phase.
Deposed by her husband, marking instability before Hilaaly rise.
people.no: Daughter of #26 and #25 (Raadhaafathi). RoyalArk confirms mother is Malika Fatima (P25). Forced to abdicate by clerics who opposed female rule.
people.bio:
Dhaain, the twenty-seventh sovereign, bore the full title Malika Rehendi Dh'ainu Svasti Sri Keerithi Maha Rehendi and the pre-accession title Kamba'adi Kilege. She was the daughter of Mohamed of Maakurathu (P26) and Raadhaafathi (P25), and thus the granddaughter of Omar Veeru (P18) through her mother.

Dhaain acceded before February 12, 1385, and married Abdullah II (P28). Her reign of approximately three years ended not through death or voluntary abdication, but through forced removal: the religious establishment compelled her to abdicate before December 31, 1388, on the grounds that female rule was unacceptable under Islamic law. This clerical intervention ended the remarkable sequence of three female sovereigns, Khadijah, Raadhaafathi, and Dhaain, that had characterized the late Lunar period. The forced abdication marked a turning point in Maldivian political culture, establishing a de facto prohibition on female sovereignty that would endure for the remainder of the monarchy.

people.no: Husband of #27.
people.bio:
Abdullah II, the twenty-eighth sovereign, bore the regnal name Suvama Abarana and the title Sultan Abdullah II Svasti Sri Somu Abaruna Maha Radhun. He was the husband of Dhaain (P27) and came to power through her forced abdication before December 31, 1388.

Abdullah's reign was one of the briefest in Maldivian history, lasting only one month. Neither his parentage nor his dynasty is specified in the surviving sources, and no children are recorded. He was quickly supplanted by Osman of Fehendu (P29), whose own brief reign would in turn give way to the Hilaaly dynasty's seizure of power. Abdullah's one-month tenure illustrates the extreme political instability of the transition between the late Lunar period and the Hilaaly era.

facts: Last Lunar-era ruler before Hilaaly consolidation.
Also known as a political operator serving as PM to earlier rulers.
people.no: PM to #25 and #27.
people.bio:
Osman of Fehendu, the twenty-ninth sovereign, bore the regnal name Sundhura and the title Sultan Usman al-Fehendu. He was the last effective ruler of the Lunar era, serving as what the chronicles describe as a "PM" (prime minister or power-broker) to Raadhaafathi (P25) and Dhaain (P27) before briefly holding the throne himself.

Osman's reign lasted only six months and fifteen days before he was deposed by Hassan al-Hilali (P30), the founder of the Hilaaly dynasty. After his deposition, Osman was exiled to Guraidu in Kolhumadulu Atoll, where he lived until his death around 1425. His son Osman II (P37) later held the throne briefly in 1421, suggesting that the Fehendu family retained political relevance even after their displacement. Osman's deposition in 1388 marks the definitive end of the Lunar dynasty's control over the Maldivian succession.

facts: Founder of the Hilaaly dynasty.
Traditionally linked to the Golaavahi maternal line and Hilaaly paternal line.
Founder of the Hilaaly dynasty. Father was Dori Kuja (P217), also known as Kuliveri Hilalu Kaivulanna Kaloge of Hulule, North Male Atoll.
Grandfather Abbas al-Hilal was of Malabar (Indian) origin, giving the dynasty its name.
Twin brother of Hussain (P32). Married Golavehi Kabulo (P218).
Four sons became sultans: Ibrahim I (P31), Yusuf II (P39), Abu Bakar I (P40), and possibly Osman II.
Deposed Osman of Fehendu (P29) to establish the new dynasty.
people.no: Hilaaly Dynasty founder.

known_as.name: Sultan Hassan al-Hilali Sri Dhiru Buwana Maha Radhun
known_as.note: Full Arabic and Sanskritic title.

people.bio:
Hassan al-Hilali, the thirtieth sovereign and founder of the Hilaaly dynasty, transformed Maldivian political history by deposing the last Lunar-era ruler Osman of Fehendu (P29) and establishing a new royal house that would rule the archipelago for over two centuries. His full title, Sultan Hassan al-Hilali Sri Dhiru Buwana Maha Radhun, combined Arabic patronymic with Sanskritic regnal style in the characteristic manner of Maldivian royal nomenclature.

Hassan's origins were distinctly foreign by Maldivian standards. His father was Dori Kuja, also known as Kuliveri Hilalu Kaivulanna Kaloge, a nobleman of Hulule in North Male Atoll. His paternal grandfather, Abbas al-Hilal, was of Malabar (Indian) origin, and it was from this grandfather's name that the Hilaaly dynasty derived its designation. The Malabar connection placed the Hilaaly house within the broader networks of Indian Muslim merchant and aristocratic families who had been migrating across the Indian Ocean for centuries.

Hassan married Golavehi Kabulo (P218) and fathered four sons who would all play roles in the dynastic succession: Ibrahim I (P31), who succeeded him but was soon deposed; Yusuf II (P39), who enjoyed the longest Hilaaly reign; Abu Bakar I (P40); and possibly a son named Usman. Remarkably, Hassan had a twin brother, Hussain (P32), who would usurp the throne from Hassan's young son Ibrahim I in 1398, adding a fraternal rivalry to the complex Hilaaly succession politics.

Hassan deposed Osman of Fehendu after Osman's brief six-month reign, exiling the last Lunar-era figure to Guraidu in Kolhumadulu Atoll. He died around 1397-1398, and the succession immediately became contested between his young son Ibrahim I and his twin brother Hussain. The Hilaaly dynasty Hassan founded would survive through constant internal warfare, multiple depositions, and foreign interventions until its final extinction in the Portuguese period, leaving an indelible mark on Maldivian royal genealogy.

people.no: Son of #30. Deposed by uncle, later returned.
people.bio:
Ibrahim I al-Hilali, the thirty-first sovereign, bore the regnal name Dhammaru Veeru and the full title Sultan Ibrahim I al-Hilali Sultan Hassan Svasti Sri Dhammara Vira Maha Radun. He was the eldest son of Hassan al-Hilali (P30), the Hilaaly dynasty founder.

Ibrahim's career was marked by two widely separated reigns. His first accession, on September 24, 1397, came as a youth under his uncle Hussain's (P32) regency. The uncle quickly deposed his nephew on September 13, 1398, seizing the throne for himself. Ibrahim's second reign, beginning on July 23, 1411, after the turbulent interregnum of Nasiruddine, Hassan II, and Isa, proved more durable. He held power until his death before January 17, 1420, a period of approximately nine years.

Ibrahim's two accessions illustrate the factional dynamics within the early Hilaaly dynasty, where the founder's sons and grandsons competed for supremacy. His ultimate restoration suggests that the legitimacy conferred by primogeniture retained significant political weight, even in a system that tolerated usurpation by powerful uncles.

people.no: Twin brother of #30.
people.bio:
Hussain al-Hilali, the thirty-second sovereign of the Maldives, was the twin brother of Hassan al-Hilali (P30), the founder of the Hilaaly dynasty. His full title, Sultan Husain al-Hilali Svasti Sri Loka Veeru Maha Radun, combined the Arabic patronymic with the Sanskritic regnal style characteristic of Maldivian sultans. Like his twin, Hussain was a son of Dori Kuja (P217) and grandson of Abbas al-Hilal of Malabar.

Hussain came to power by usurping the throne from his young nephew Ibrahim I (P31), Hassan's son, in 1398. The deposition of a child sultan by his uncle and guardian was a familiar pattern in medieval Islamic polities, and Hussain justified his seizure of power by acting as regent-turned-ruler. He married Kabafa'anu Rani Kilege and fathered at least one daughter, Ma'afai Kalo Kilege.

Hussain ruled for approximately eleven years until his death before May 17, 1409. His reign provided stability to the nascent Hilaaly dynasty during its critical early years, even as it established the pattern of fratricidal succession conflict that would characterize Hilaaly politics for the next century. After his death, the throne was briefly seized by the outsider Nasiruddine (P33) before Ibrahim I was eventually restored. Through his descendants, Hussain's branch of the Hilaaly family continued to contest for power, with Ali IV (P53) later tracing his claim through Hussain's lineage.

people.no: Probably Lunar Dynasty.
people.bio:
Nasiruddine, the thirty-third sovereign, bore the regnal name Veeru Abarana and the full title Sultan Nasir ud-din al-Kalavahi Svasti Sri Vira Abaruna Maha Radun. His pre-accession title was Gola'avahi Nasir ud-din. Unlike the Hilaaly sultans who preceded and followed him, Nasiruddine's origins were foreign: he arrived from Chittagong, in modern Bangladesh, as a boy.

Nasiruddine usurped the throne after Hussain al-Hilali's (P32) death in 1409. His reign was extraordinarily brief, lasting only approximately three months before he was killed or deposed around August 1409. He left one unnamed daughter, who briefly held power with her husband as Grand Vizier before being deposed by Hassan II (P34). His Chittagong origins are a reminder that the medieval Maldivian court, positioned at the crossroads of Indian Ocean trade routes, attracted adventurers and claimants from across the Muslim world.

people.no: Drowned. Son of Maayin Rannabandeyri Kilege.
people.bio:
Hassan II, the thirty-fourth sovereign, bore the regnal name Keerithi Maha Radun and the title Hassan II ibnu Mayin Svasti Sri Dhammara Buwana Maha Radun. He was the son of Maayin Rannabandeyri Kilege (P206), whose name identifies him as holding the Rannabandeyri court office.

Hassan II deposed the daughter of Nasiruddine before August 3, 1410, and reigned for approximately nine months. His death was violent and unusual: he drowned in the palace tank, an event that the chronicles imply was murder rather than accident. He was succeeded by his younger brother Isa (P35), indicating that the brothers from the P206 line briefly held power as a pair before being displaced by the restoration of Ibrahim I (P31).

people.no: Younger brother of #34.
people.bio:
Isa, the thirty-fifth sovereign, bore the regnal name Bavana Sundhura and the title Isa ibnu Mayin Svasti Sri Buwana Sundara Maha Radun. He was the younger brother of Hassan II (P34) and, like his brother, a son of Maayin Rannabandeyri Kilege (P206).

Isa succeeded his murdered brother and reigned for only three months before July 23, 1411, when he was deposed by the restoration of Ibrahim I (P31). The brevity of both brothers' reigns, nine months and three months respectively, underscores their tenuous hold on power. The P206 line appears to have been a minor branch of the Hilaaly family or a connected noble house that seized a fleeting opportunity during the interregnum between Hussain's death and Ibrahim's restoration.

people.no: Son of #29.
people.bio:
Osman II, the thirty-seventh sovereign, bore the regnal name Dhammaru Loaka. The chronicles identify his father as Osman of Fehendu (P29), the last Lunar-era ruler, though RoyalArk attributes this sovereign number to a son of Hassan al-Hilali (P30). This discrepancy likely reflects the conflation of two different Osmans in the sources.

Osman II's reign was extremely brief, lasting only three months around 1421. He died before April 17, 1420, according to the RoyalArk chronology. His placement in the succession between the second reign of Ibrahim I (P31) and the reign of Danna Mohamed (P38) occurred during a period of rapid turnover, when the Hilaaly dynasty was still consolidating its hold on power and multiple branches competed for the throne.

people.no: Son of Yusuf Handeygirin (paternal uncle of #30 and #32).
people.bio:
Danna Mohamed, the thirty-eighth sovereign, bore the regnal name Raadha Bavana and the full title Sultan Danna Muhammad ibnu Khadim Yusuf Svasi Sri Raja Buwana Maha Radun. His father was Khadim Ma'a Banderi Yusuf Handegiri, a paternal uncle of Hassan al-Hilali (P30), making Danna Mohamed a cousin of the Hilaaly founder.

Before his accession, Danna Mohamed served as Fadiyaru, a religious judicial office, under Sultan Hussain I (P32). He was described in the chronicles as "very pious, learned and noble soul" and had been educated in Addu Atoll. He married Amina Kabafa'anu and had one daughter, Tukkabafa'anu, who was rejected as a successor on grounds of gender.

Danna Mohamed's brief reign, from before April 17, 1420, to his deposition before January 6, 1421, lasted less than a year. His deposition and his daughter's exclusion from the succession reinforced the male-preference norms that the clerical establishment had imposed after the forced abdication of Dhaain (P27). His cousin-branch connection to the Hilaaly founders demonstrates the extended kinship networks through which political power was contested in fifteenth-century Male.

people.no: Son of #30.
people.bio:
Yoosuf II, the thirty-ninth sovereign and a son of dynasty founder Hassan al-Hilali (P30), presided over the longest stable reign of the early Hilaaly period, ruling from approximately 1421 to 1443. His accession came after a turbulent decade that had seen six sultans rise and fall in rapid succession following the death of his uncle Hussain (P32).

The most notable event of Yoosuf's reign was the reception of a diplomatic embassy from the Chinese Ming Emperor Yung Lo in February 1421, placing the Maldives within the vast maritime diplomatic network of the Zheng He expeditions. This Chinese contact attests to the Maldives' continuing importance as a node in Indian Ocean trade routes, particularly for cowrie shells, coir rope, and dried fish.

Yoosuf married Amira Bulau Ma'ava Kilege, whom he later divorced; she subsequently married his brother Abu Bakar I (P40). He fathered two sons who would both become sultans: Hassan III, known as Hadi Hassan (P41), and Umar II (P46). Through these sons, Yoosuf's line dominated the mid-fifteenth-century Hilaaly succession. He died before May 2, 1443, and was succeeded first by his brother Abu Bakar I and then by his son Hadi Hassan.

people.no: Son of #30, half-brother of #39.
people.bio:
Aboobakuru I, the fortieth sovereign, bore the regnal name Bavana Sooja and the title Sultan Abu Bakar I al-Hilali. He was a son of Hassan al-Hilali (P30), the Hilaaly dynasty founder, and thus a half-brother of Yoosuf II (P39), whom he succeeded.

Abu Bakar married twice: first to Reki Kamana of Kadudu in Kolumadulu Atoll, and second to Amira Bulau Ma'ava Kilege, the former wife of his half-brother Yoosuf II. This transfer of spouses between royal brothers was a common feature of medieval Maldivian succession politics. He fathered at least two daughters who became politically significant: Princess Rekka (P207), who was the mother of Ali V (P57), and Princess Kaba Dio (P202), who was the mother of Sheikh Hassan (P49).

Abu Bakar's reign lasted only seven months, ending with his death before November 24, 1443. He was buried at the Bodugalu Mosque in Male. Although brief, his reign produced genealogical connections that shaped subsequent Hilaaly politics: through his daughters, Abu Bakar's line contributed to the succession for several generations.

people.no: Son of #39 (Yoosuf II) per RoyalArk. Two accessions. Was Qazi before becoming sultan. Made two Hajj pilgrimages (1454, 1466).
people.bio:
Hadi Hassan III, the forty-first sovereign, was one of the most devout and well-traveled rulers of the Hilaaly dynasty, holding the throne across two accessions (1443-1467 and 1467-1468). His full title, Sultan Hassan III Nasir ul-Mukhatib Hassan Svasti Sri Raadha Veeru Maha Radun, reflects both his Arabic religious credentials and his traditional Maldivian regnal style. He was a son of Yoosuf II (P39), according to RoyalArk genealogies.

Before ascending the throne, Hadi Hassan served as Qazi (chief judge) under Sultan Danna Mohamed (P38), an appointment that reflects his religious learning and juridical authority. He was also known for his personal piety: he undertook two pilgrimages to Mecca, the first in 1454 and the second in 1466. The second Hajj journey was particularly eventful; he returned with seventy slaves, a detail preserved in the chronicles that illuminates the Indian Ocean slave trade routes connecting Arabia and the Maldives.

Hadi Hassan married Golararu Golavehi Sanfa'a Rani Kilege and fathered Muhammad al-Hafiz (P44), who would succeed him. His second accession came about because of the Arab interloper Sayyid Mohamed (P42), who seized the throne during Hadi Hassan's absence on pilgrimage in 1467. Upon his return, Hadi Hassan deposed the usurper and reclaimed his throne, but survived only briefly, dying before July 11, 1469 from what the chronicles describe as an "agonizing disease."

people.no: An Arab. Deposed by #41.
people.bio:
Sayyid Mohamed, the forty-second sovereign, bore the regnal name Keerithi Maha Radun. He was an Arab whose specific parentage and origins are not recorded in the surviving Maldivian chronicles. He seized power during the second Hajj pilgrimage of Hadi Hassan (P41) in 1467.

Sayyid Mohamed's usurpation represents one of several instances in Maldivian history when an Arab outsider exploited a succession vacuum. His reign lasted only months before Hadi Hassan returned from pilgrimage and recovered the throne. The episode illustrates both the vulnerability of the sultanate during royal absences and the continuing presence of Arab adventurers and scholars at the Maldivian court, a legacy of the Indian Ocean trade networks that connected the archipelago to Arabia, Persia, and beyond.

people.no: Son of #41.
people.bio:
Mohamed, the forty-fourth sovereign, bore the regnal name Bavana Abarana and the full title Sultan Muhammad al-Hafiz Svasti Sri Bavana Abarana Maha Radun. He was the son of Hadi Hassan (P41) and married Golavehi Rani Kilege. Their son, Hassan IV (P45), continued the succession.

Mohamed had served as regent during his father's second Hajj pilgrimage in 1466, giving him experience of governance before his own accession after Hadi Hassan's death. His twelve-year reign, from 1468 until his death on September 23, 1479, brought stability to the Hilaaly succession after the disruptions of the Sayyid Mohamed usurpation. He was buried at Lonu Ziyarat in Male. Through his son Hassan IV and his brother Omar II (P46), Mohamed's generation established the two main competing branches of the later Hilaaly dynasty.

people.no: Son of #44. Two accessions.
people.bio:
Hassan IV, the forty-fifth sovereign, bore the regnal name Raadha Loka and the title Sultan Hassan IV Svasti Sri Raadha Loka Maha Radun. He was the son of Mohamed al-Hafiz (P44) and grandson of Hadi Hassan (P41).

Hassan's two accessions were both marked by political instability. His first reign in 1480 lasted only one month before his grand-uncle Omar II (P46) deposed him. His second reign, from approximately 1486 to 1490, came after the death of Hassan V (P47), Omar's son. He died before November 14, 1490. The ease with which Hassan was deposed and restored illustrates the factional nature of late Hilaaly politics, in which the descendants of Yoosuf II through his two sons, Hadi Hassan and Omar, competed relentlessly for the throne.

people.no: Son of #39.
people.bio:
Omar II, the forty-sixth sovereign, bore the regnal name Loka Sundhura and the title Sultan Umar II Svasti Sri Loka Sundhura Maha Radun. He was a son of Yoosuf II (P39) and thus a younger brother or half-brother of Hadi Hassan (P41), representing a rival branch within the Hilaaly royal house.

Omar deposed his grand-nephew Hassan IV (P45) in October 1479 and reigned until his death before January 18, 1485. He married three times: first to Amira Ma'ava Kilege of Fehendu, second to Amira Laru Ma'ava Kilege, and third to Sitti Rani Kilege, a maternal half-sister of Abu Bakar I (P40). These marriages produced three sons who all became sultans: Hassan V (P47) through his first or second wife, Yoosuf III (P52) through his second wife, and Kalu Mohamed (P51) through Sitti Rani Kilege.

Omar's prolific issue fundamentally shaped the late Hilaaly succession. The rivalry between his sons, and between his line and the line of Hadi Hassan, produced the chaotic sequence of depositions and restorations that characterized the final century of the Hilaaly dynasty.

people.no: Son of #46.
people.bio:
Hassan V, the forty-seventh sovereign, bore the regnal name Raadha Aanandha. He was a son of Omar II (P46) and married Fatima Rani Kilege. Their son, Ibrahim II (P50), continued the succession.

Hassan's brief reign from approximately 1484 to 1485 ended with his death before December 28, 1486. He succeeded his father but was unable to establish a durable hold on power. After his death, the throne passed briefly to his cousin Hassan IV (P45) for a second time, before the factional struggles among Omar's sons erupted into the series of depositions that characterized the 1490s. Hassan V's son Ibrahim II (P50) would himself reign only briefly before being deposed by his uncle Kalu Mohamed (P51).

people.no: Son of Kamba Dio (daughter of #40).
people.bio:
Sheikh Hassan VI, the forty-ninth sovereign, bore the regnal name Raadha Fanaveeru and the title Sultan Hassan VI Svasti Sri Raadha Fanaveeru Maha Radun. His father was Talamedi Kilege, and his mother was Kamba Dio (P202), a daughter of Abu Bakar I (P40), giving him a matrilineal connection to the Hilaaly royal house.

Before his accession, Sheikh Hassan was known as a scholar and religious figure, one of several Hilaaly-era sultans who combined religious learning with political authority. He came to power after the death of Hassan IV (P45) and reigned briefly, dying before June 9, 1491. His title "Sheikh" distinguishes him as a man of religious learning, and his accession through the maternal line via P202 demonstrates the continuing importance of matrilineal connections in the Hilaaly succession.

people.no: Son of #47 (Hassan V), grandson of #46. Deposed after 8 months by P51.
people.bio:
Ibrahim II, the fiftieth sovereign, bore the regnal name Bavana Furasuddha. He was the son of Hassan V (P47) and the grandson of Omar II (P46). He came to power after the death of Sheikh Hassan (P49) and reigned for approximately eight months before being deposed by his uncle Kalu Mohamed (P51) before January 31, 1492.

Ibrahim's brief reign and subsequent deposition by his uncle exemplified the factional violence within the house of Omar II. As a son of Omar's eldest son, Ibrahim had a strong hereditary claim, but political power in the late Hilaaly period was determined more by military capability and factional alliance than by primogeniture. His deposition inaugurated the tumultuous career of Kalu Mohamed, who would seize and lose the throne three times.

people.no: Son of #46. Three accessions.
facts: Three separate accessions spanning 1492-1529, making him one of the most persistent claimants to the Hilaaly throne.

known_as.name: Kalu Bandaarain
known_as.note: Popular historical name for Sultan Kalu Mohamed; widely used in chronicles and secondary literature.

people.bio:
Kalu Mohamed, the most tenacious claimant to the Hilaaly throne, ruled the Maldives across three separate accessions spanning nearly four decades (1492-1529), making him one of the most persistent political survivors in the archipelago's history. His popular sobriquet "Kalu Bandaarain" remained his enduring identifier in the chronicle traditions. His pre-accession title, Raddeba Magu Kalu Muhammad Manikufa'anu, places him within the Hilaaly aristocratic naming conventions.

Kalu Mohamed was a son of Omar II (P46) by his third wife Sitti Rani Kilege, who was herself the maternal half-sister of Abu Bakar I (P40), creating a dense web of Hilaaly kinship connections. His first accession in 1492 lasted only nine months before he was deposed by his half-brother Yusuf III (P52). After Yusuf's death and the brief intervening reign of Ali IV (P53), Kalu Mohamed secured the throne a second time around 1496 and held it for approximately fifteen years until deposed by his nephew Hassan VII (P55) in 1510.

His third and final reign, beginning around 1514, was achieved with the military support of the Ali Raja of Cannanore and the Portuguese, inaugurating an era of increasing foreign entanglement in Maldivian politics. The Portuguese demanded annual tribute as the price of their support, marking the beginning of European commercial and political penetration of the archipelago. Kalu Mohamed married four wives in succession: an unnamed first wife, Buraki Rani (a daughter of P40 known for her martial arts prowess), Fatima Dio from Shiraz in Persia, and Amira Aisatu Kilege.

Kalu Mohamed died before August 25, 1530. His children shaped the next generation of Maldivian politics profoundly: his son Hassan VIII as-Shirazi (P59) by Fatima Dio succeeded him directly; his sons Umar Ma'afai Kilege (P219) and Ahmad Manikufa'anu Kalaminja (P220) fathered Mohamed (P60) and Hassan IX/Dom Manoel (P61) respectively; and his daughter Princess Aysha Rani Kilege (P208) married Ali VI (P63). Through these descendants, Kalu Mohamed's bloodline persisted through the Portuguese interregnum, the Utheemu liberation, and ultimately into the Huraagey dynasty that ruled the Maldives until 1968.

people.no: Son of #46.
people.bio:
Yoosuf III, the fifty-second sovereign, bore the regnal name Veeru Aanandha. He was a son of Omar II (P46) by his second wife Amira Laru Ma'ava Kilege, and he married Sanfa'a Rani Kilege. Their son, Hassan VII (P55), would also briefly hold the throne.

Yoosuf deposed his brother Kalu Mohamed (P51) during the latter's first reign in 1492 and held power for approximately two and a half months before dying before February 3, 1493. The extreme brevity of his reign, combined with the fratricide and depositions that characterized this period, illustrates the near-total breakdown of orderly succession among the sons of Omar II. Yoosuf's line continued through his son Hassan VII, who deposed Kalu Mohamed during the latter's second reign around 1510.

people.no: Deposed by #51. Descendant of #32.
people.bio:
Ali IV, the fifty-third sovereign, bore the regnal name Audha Veeru. His father was Kaulanna'a Kalo Kilege, and his lineage traced back to Hussain al-Hilali (P32), the twin brother of the Hilaaly dynasty founder. This genealogical connection made Ali a representative of the Hussain branch of the Hilaaly family, which had been largely eclipsed by the descendants of Hassan al-Hilali since the early fifteenth century.

Ali's reign of approximately two years and four months, from 1493 to his deposition before September 9, 1496, ended when Kalu Mohamed (P51) recovered the throne for his second accession. Ali's brief period of rule demonstrates that the Hussain branch retained political ambitions and sufficient support to mount a credible challenge, even though the main Hilaaly line descended through Hassan al-Hilali would ultimately prevail.

people.no: Son of #52.
people.bio:
Hassan VII, the fifty-fifth sovereign, bore the regnal name Singa Veeru. He was the son of Yoosuf III (P52) and Sanfa'a Rani Kilege. He came to power by deposing his uncle Kalu Mohamed (P51) during the latter's second reign, after April 10, 1510.

Hassan's reign lasted less than a year, ending with his death before March 31, 1511. His brief hold on power was part of the ongoing struggle between the sons and grandsons of Omar II (P46) for control of the Hilaaly succession. After his death, the throne passed to Sharif Ahmed (P56), an Arab from Mecca, suggesting that no member of the immediate Hilaaly family was positioned to claim the throne immediately.

people.no: An Arab from Mecca.
people.bio:
Sharif Ahmed, the fifty-sixth sovereign, bore the regnal name Suddha Bavana and the title Sultan Ahmad Svasti Sri Suddha Bavana Maha Radun. His pre-accession title was Sharif Ahmad Rasge, identifying him as a descendant of the Prophet Muhammad. He was born in Mecca and belonged to the broader Arab scholarly-merchant community that maintained a presence at the Maldivian court.

Sharif Ahmed was nominated by an Arab court faction after the death of Hassan VII (P55), in circumstances that suggest the Hilaaly princely families were too fractured to produce an immediate consensus candidate. His reign of approximately two years and nine months, ending before November 29, 1513, was modest in duration. He was the second Arab outsider to hold the Maldivian throne, after Sayyid Mohamed (P42), and the last until the Syrian Mohamed Shamsuddine (P72) in 1692.

people.no: Mother Recca is daughter of #40.
people.bio:
Ali V, the fifty-seventh sovereign, bore the regnal name Aanandha. His pre-accession title was Al-Amir Dombula Ali Farina Kilege. His father was Al-Amir Muhammad Farina Kilege Kalo Mavadi Koya, and his mother was Princess Recca (P207), a daughter of Abu Bakar I (P40), giving him a matrilineal connection to the Hilaaly royal house.

Ali's reign of approximately nine months in 1513 ended violently: he was killed before February 27, 1514, by an assassin named Huludali Don Yusuf, acting at the instigation of Ali's own sister Buraki Rani. The chronicles describe Buraki Rani as a martial arts practitioner who had fled to Aceh in Southeast Asia. Her instigation of her brother's assassination cleared the way for Kalu Mohamed's (P51) third and final accession to the throne. Ali's wife was Princess Aysha Rani Kilege (P208), a daughter of Kalu Mohamed, an alliance that evidently did not prevent the deadly factional politics of the period.

people.no: Son of Kalu Mohamed (#51).
people.bio:
Hassan VIII as-Shirazi, the fifty-ninth sovereign, bore the regnal name Ram Mani Loka. He was the son of Kalu Mohamed (P51) by his Persian wife Fatima Dio of Shiraz, from whom the sobriquet "as-Shirazi" derived. This Shirazi connection placed Hassan within the broader network of Persian-Indian Ocean cultural exchange that had shaped the Maldives since the conversion era.

Hassan succeeded his father around 1529 and reigned for approximately twenty years, the longest continuous reign in the Hilaaly dynasty after Yoosuf II (P39). He died before January 17, 1547, according to RoyalArk, though the project gives his reign end as 1549. His two decades of rule brought relative stability to the Hilaaly succession after the chaotic depositions and restorations that had characterized his father's era. He was succeeded by Mohamed (P60), a grandson of Kalu Mohamed through a different son, marking the continuation of the Hilaaly dynasty into its final, Portuguese-influenced phase.

people.no: Grandson of #51. Assassinated by brother.
people.bio:
Mohamed, the sixtieth sovereign of the Maldives, reigned briefly from 1549 to 1551 during one of the most volatile periods in the archipelago's history. He was a grandson of Kalu Mohamed (P51) through an intermediate figure, Prince Umar Ma'afai Kilege, and his mother was Golavehi Aisha Rani Kilege.

His short reign ended in violence when he was assassinated by his own half-brother Hassan, who shared the same mother but had a different father -- Ahmad Manikufa'anu Kalaminja, another son of Kalu Mohamed. Hassan would go on to become the ninth sultan of that name (P61) and, after fleeing to Cochin, the Catholic convert Dom Manoel. Mohamed's murder thus set in motion one of the most dramatic sequences of events in Maldivian royal history, leading to the Portuguese interregnum and ultimately to the liberation by Mohamed Thakurufaanu two decades later.

facts: Converted to Christianity January 1, 1552 at Cochin under Portuguese protection.
Associated with rule in absentia from Goa through regents.
Same individual across accessions #61, #64, and #65.
Born 1531 at Male (RoyalArk); earlier project data had yb:1525. Died before July 10, 1584 at Cochin (RoyalArk); earlier data had yd:1585.
Usurped the throne from his half-brother before May 1549; fled to Cochin in 1551 after two years and nine months of reign.
Converted to Catholicism on January 1, 1552, at Cochin under Portuguese protection, taking the name Dom Manoel.
Married twice: first to Dona Maria (a Christian convert, died before 1553), then to Dona Leonor de Ataide (1554, at Goa), daughter of Dom Heitor de Sousa de Ataide.
Restored by Portuguese force on May 19, 1558, but remained in Cochin/Goa while the islands were governed by the regent Andiri Andirin (Andreas Andre).
Had at least five children: Dom Francisco (P209, killed in Lisbon), Dom Joao (P66), Dom Pedro (P210), Dona Leonor (P211), and Dona Catarina (P212).
The Portuguese period ended in 1573 when Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104) liberated the Maldives.
Birth date contradicted: project data says 1525, RoyalArk gives 1531. Death date contradicted: project says 1585, RoyalArk says before July 10, 1584. See CLOG-2026-02-09-D1.
people.no: Converted to Christianity at Cochin 1552. Ruled in absentia from Goa via regents. Same person as sovereign #61, #64, #65.

known_as.name: Dom Manoel
known_as.note: Portuguese baptismal name adopted upon Catholic conversion at Cochin on January 1, 1552.

known_as.name: Hassan IX
known_as.note: Ninth Hassan in the Maldivian sovereign list (accession #61, #64, #65).

known_as.name: Svasi Sri Dhirukusalokya Maha Radun
known_as.note: Full regnal style at initial accession before Portuguese conversion.

people.bio:
Hassan IX, better known by his Portuguese baptismal name Dom Manoel, stands as one of the most dramatically transfigured figures in Maldivian history. Born in Male around 1531, he was a grandson of the formidable Kalu Mohamed (P51) through his father Ahmad Manikufa'anu Kalaminja. His mother, Golavehi Aisha Rani Kilege, had previously been married to Umar Ma'afai Kilege, another son of Kalu Mohamed, making Hassan IX a half-brother of Sultan Mohamed (P60) through their shared mother but different fathers.

Hassan seized the throne from his half-brother before May 1549, taking the traditional Maldivian regnal style Svasi Sri Dhirukusalokya Maha Radun. His reign in Male lasted barely two years and nine months before political opposition forced him to flee to Cochin on the Malabar Coast, placing himself under Portuguese protection. It was there, on January 1, 1552, that he underwent one of the most consequential religious conversions in Indian Ocean history, accepting Catholic baptism and the name Dom Manoel.

The conversion was more than a personal spiritual transformation; it was a calculated political alliance. The Portuguese Estado da India, headquartered in Goa, saw in Dom Manoel a pliant client ruler who could extend their influence over the strategically located Maldive archipelago. In 1554, this alliance was cemented by his marriage to Dona Leonor de Ataide, daughter of the Portuguese nobleman Dom Heitor de Sousa de Ataide, at Goa. His first wife, Dona Maria, a Christian convert, had died before 1553. Together with Dona Leonor he would have at least five children: Dom Francisco (P209), Dom Joao (P66), Dom Pedro (P210), Dona Leonor (P211), and Dona Catarina (P212).

On May 19, 1558, the Portuguese restored Dom Manoel to the Maldivian throne by force, but in a remarkable arrangement he never returned to govern his islands in person. Instead, he remained in Cochin and Goa while the Maldives were administered by the regent Andiri Andirin, described in Maldivian sources as a "Goa Kalu Faranji" -- a dark-skinned Christian from Goa. This period of rule-in-absentia through a Christian regent is remembered in Maldivian national memory as the era of Portuguese domination, though the extent of actual Portuguese colonial control remains historically contested.

The Portuguese interregnum ended in 1573 when Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104) and his brothers launched their celebrated guerrilla campaign of liberation, overthrowing the regent and ending Christian governance of the islands. Dom Manoel continued to claim the Maldivian throne from exile in Goa, and his nominal sovereignty is counted as accession number sixty-five in the traditional king-lists. He died at Cochin before July 10, 1584, never having set foot in his kingdom again.

Dom Manoel's historical significance extends far beyond his own lifetime. Through his son Dom Joao (P66) and granddaughter Dona Ines (P213), his bloodline would eventually produce the Huraagey dynasty that ruled the Maldives from 1759 until the abolition of the monarchy in 1968. The genealogical bridge connecting a Catholic convert king in sixteenth-century Goa to the last sultans of the twentieth century is one of the most extraordinary lineage threads in Indian Ocean royal history.

people.no: PM to #61.
people.bio:
Aboobakuru, the sixty-second sovereign, served as a caretaker ruler from 1554 to 1557 during the chaotic period following Dom Manoel's flight to Cochin. Previously the prime minister to Hassan IX (P61), Aboobakuru assumed the throne in the power vacuum left by the sultan's departure. His dynasty is recorded as unknown, suggesting he may have been a capable administrator rather than a member of the established royal bloodlines.

His brief reign bridged the gap between Dom Manoel's first accession and the Portuguese restoration of 1558. Little is recorded of his governance, but his ability to hold power for three years in a period of extreme political instability speaks to some degree of political skill or factional support.

people.no: Killed in battle. Married Princess Aysha Rani Kilege, daughter of #51.
facts: Head-of-state chronologies describe him as Ali VI (Dhevvadhoo Rasgefaanu).

known_as.name: Dhevvadhoo Rasgefaanu
known_as.note: Listed as an alternate known-by name in head-of-state chronology.

people.bio:
Ali VI, known by the sobriquet Dhevvadhoo Rasgefaanu, briefly held the Maldivian throne from 1557 to 1558 during the turbulent years following Dom Manoel's flight to Cochin. He had married Princess Aysha Rani Kilege (P208), a daughter of the powerful Kalu Mohamed (P51), which gave him a claim to legitimacy through the Hilaaly royal house.

His reign was cut short when he was killed in battle, likely in the factional violence that characterized the interregnum between the old Hilaaly order and the emerging Portuguese-backed regime. His death cleared the way for the Portuguese restoration of Hassan IX (Dom Manoel) in 1558, ushering in the period of rule from Goa through appointed regents.

facts: Son of Dom Manoel (Hassan IX).
Ancestor of the later Huraagey founder line through Donna Ines.
people.no: Son of Manoel I.
people.bio:
Dom Joao, the titular king of the Maldives from 1583 to approximately 1603, was born around 1560 at Cochin as the son of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. He grew up entirely within the Portuguese colonial world, never knowing the Maldivian islands his father claimed to rule. Upon Dom Manoel's death before July 10, 1584, Dom Joao was proclaimed King of the Maldive Islands under his regnal style Sri Kithiri Maha Radun.

His conduct in Goa was anything but regal. Portuguese authorities described his behavior as "so outrageous and scandalous" that the Viceroy was compelled to place him under surveillance. He was eventually removed to Goa from Cochin in 1591. Before November 23, 1587, he had married Dona Francisca de Vasconcelos (P101), sister of Captain Antonio Teixeira de Macedo, at Cochin.

Dom Joao's importance to Maldivian dynastic history far exceeds his personal achievements. He fathered two children who would shape the future of the royal succession: Dom Philippe (P67) and, crucially, Infanta Dona Ines (P213). It was through Dona Ines, not through her brother, that the Hilaaly royal bloodline would pass into the Huraagey dynasty, making Dom Joao the essential link between the Portuguese-era exile monarchs and the dynasty that would rule the Maldives for its final two centuries as a sultanate.

facts: Christian-period Hilaaly ruler in the Goa-linked branch.
Unmarried, died without issue (s.p. unm.) per RoyalArk.
Knight of the Order of Christ (1618).
Huraagey lineage passes through sister Dona Ines (P213), not through P67.
people.no: Son of #66. Died unmarried without children. Huraagey bridge goes through sister P213.
people.bio:
Dom Philippe, born in 1593 at Goa as Dom Felipe de Malvidas, was the last male-line representative of the Portuguese-era Hilaaly kings. He succeeded his father Dom Joao (P66) as titular King of the Maldive Islands around 1604, bearing the regnal style Sri Kathiri Maha Radun. In 1618 he was honored with the Knight of the Order of Christ, a Portuguese royal distinction that underscored his family's integration into Iberian aristocratic society.

Dom Philippe's significance to Maldivian genealogy lies precisely in what he did not accomplish: he died unmarried and without issue before 1639 at Goa. The absence of a male heir meant that the Hilaaly royal bloodline, which had flowed through the male line since Dom Manoel's conversion in 1552, now had to pass through Dom Philippe's sister, Infanta Dona Ines (P213). This genealogical pivot through the female line proved to be the critical bridge that connected the Portuguese-era exiles to the Huraagey dynasty, which would eventually reclaim the Maldivian throne in 1759.

facts: First legally proclaimed Utheemu sovereign in many lists.
Represents consolidation after the Portuguese-interregnum struggles.
people.no: Utheemu Dynasty.
people.bio:
Mohamed Imaduddine I, bearing the Arabic laqab 'Imad al-Din meaning "Pillar of the Faith," was the first sultan of the Utheemu dynasty and the ruler who inaugurated the systematic adoption of Arabic honorific titles in Maldivian sovereignty. His full titulature, Sultan Shuja'at Muhammad Imad ud-din I Bodu Rasgefa'anu, combined the Arabic martial epithet Shuja'at ("Brave") with the Dhivehi royal designation Bodu Rasgefa'anu ("Great Sovereign").

His father was Al-Amir Umar Ma'afai Kilege and his mother Kaba'a Amina Ma'ava Kilege. Through the Utheemu family network he was connected to the liberation hero Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104), whose nephew's descendants eventually produced the Utheemu sultanic line. Mohamed Imaduddine married three times -- to Aisha Kabafa'anu, Aisha Rani Kilege, and Fatuma Kilage -- and fathered several children, most notably Sultan Ibrahim Iskander I (P69).
