His reign from 1632 to his death on October 22, 1648 represented the consolidation of Islamic governance after the tumultuous Portuguese period. The adoption of a formal Arabic laqab signaled a new era of religious legitimacy and closer alignment with the broader Islamic world, replacing the Sanskritic regnal naming conventions that had persisted since the pre-Islamic period.

people.no: Son of #68.
facts: Born before May 7, 1631. Mother was Aisha Kabafa'anu, first wife of P68.
Repelled Portuguese attack in 1650. Built the Male Friday Mosque (Hukuru Miskiy, 1656-1658). Erected the first minaret in the Maldives (1674).
Made Hajj pilgrimage in 1667.
Poisoned by his second wife Maryam Kabafa'anu Rani Kilege on February 28, 1687.
Maryam then acted as regent for their son P70 (Kuda Mohamed), and both died in an explosion off Bandos Island in 1691.

known_as.name: Sultan al-Ghazi Hajji Ibrahim I Iskandar al-Izz wan-Nasr
known_as.note: Complete Arabic titulature with al-Ghazi (warrior) and Hajji honorifics.

people.bio:
Ibrahim Iskander I, known by the Hellenistic throne epithet Iskandar meaning "Alexander," was one of the most consequential sultans of the Utheemu dynasty. Born before May 7, 1631, he was the son of Mohamed Imaduddine I (P68) and his first wife Aisha Kabafa'anu. His full Arabic titulature, Sultan al-Ghazi Hajji Ibrahim I Iskandar al-'Izz w'an-Nasr, proclaimed him both a warrior for the faith (al-Ghazi) and a pilgrim to Mecca (Hajji).

His thirty-nine year reign from 1648 to 1687 was marked by both military valor and cultural achievement. He successfully repelled a Portuguese naval attack in 1650, definitively ending the threat of European re-conquest. His greatest architectural legacy was the construction of the Male Friday Mosque, the Hukuru Miskiy, between 1656 and 1658, a masterpiece of coral stone craftsmanship that remains one of the finest examples of medieval Islamic architecture in the Indian Ocean. In 1674 he erected the first minaret in the Maldives, and in 1667 he undertook the Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca.

Iskander Ibrahim married twice: first to Lhaimagu Amina Kaba'afa'anu, and then to Maryam Kabafa'anu Rani Kilege, a woman who would become one of the most notorious figures in Maldivian dynastic history. On February 28, 1687, Maryam poisoned her husband, bringing his long reign to an end. She then installed their young son Kuda Mohamed (P70) as sultan and ruled as regent in his name.

Maryam's regency ended in spectacular and gruesome fashion in 1691 when both she and the child-sultan perished in an explosion aboard a vessel off Bandos Island. The circumstances of the explosion remain murky, but the deaths brought the direct Utheemu line to a crisis and inaugurated a period of rapid dynastic turnover.

people.no: Child ruler. Son of #69. Born August 24, 1680 at Male.
people.bio:
Kuda Mohamed, born on August 24, 1680 at Male, became sultan at the age of seven following the poisoning of his father Ibrahim Iskander I (P69) by his own mother, Maryam Kabafa'anu Rani Kilege, on February 28, 1687. As a child too young to govern, effective power rested entirely with Maryam, who acted as regent.

The regency was marked by the domineering influence of the queen mother, the same woman widely believed to have murdered the previous sultan. On March 5, 1691, both Kuda Mohamed and Maryam perished from burn injuries sustained in an explosion off Bandos Island. The young sultan was barely ten years old. The simultaneous death of the child-king and his regent mother brought the direct Utheemu succession line to a catastrophic end, triggering a period of rapid dynastic instability that saw five different sultans from four different dynasties in the space of thirteen years.

facts: Among the last Utheemu rulers before transition to short-lived successor houses.
people.no: Fundamentalist Muslim.
people.bio:
Mohamed Mohyedine, bearing the Arabic laqab Muhyi al-Din meaning "Reviver of the Faith," reigned for less than a year from 1691 to 1692. His father was Hassan Farina Kilege Dorimena, a grandson of Mohamed Imaduddine I (P68) through a collateral line, and his mother was Daravandu Khadija Dio. His connection to the Utheemu dynasty was thus indirect, passing through a daughter's marriage rather than direct patrilineal descent.

Described in some sources as a religious rigorist, Mohyedine briefly married the infamous Maryam Kabafa'anu before divorcing her. He died on February 18, 1692, from illness, having reigned barely eleven months. His death opened the door to Mohamed Shamsuddine (P72), his Syrian-born mentor, who was proclaimed sultan the very next day.

people.no: Arab mentor of predecessor.
facts: Born in Hama, Syria. Father was Sayyid Abdur-Razzaq al-Hamawi; mother was Sayyida Khadija.
Educated at Al-Azhar University, Cairo.
Proclaimed sultan one day after P71's death (February 19, 1692). Died July 10, 1692 from dysentery.
Married the infamous Maryam Kabafa'anu shortly before his death — the same woman who allegedly poisoned P69 and acted as regent for P70.

known_as.name: Sultan Muhammad Shams ud-din Zarb fi'l-Mahli
known_as.note: Complete Arabic title. Born in Hama, Syria.

people.bio:
Mohamed Shamsuddine, bearing the laqab Shams al-Din meaning "Sun of the Faith," was one of the most unusual figures ever to sit on the Maldivian throne. Born in Hama, Syria, he was the son of Sayyid Abdur-Razzaq al-Hamawi and Sayyida Khadija. Educated at Al-Azhar University in Cairo, he had come to the Maldives as a religious scholar and served as the spiritual mentor to his predecessor Mohamed Mohyedine (P71).

Proclaimed sultan one day after Mohyedine's death on February 19, 1692, his reign lasted barely five months before he succumbed to dysentery on July 10, 1692. In one of the more bizarre footnotes of Maldivian dynastic history, he too married the notorious Maryam Kabafa'anu shortly before his death -- the same woman who had allegedly poisoned Ibrahim Iskander (P69) and perished with Kuda Mohamed (P70). Maryam's serial marriages to successive sultans remain one of the most enigmatic patterns in the late Utheemu succession.

facts: Founder and principal ruler of the brief Devadu dynasty phase.
people.no: Devadu Dynasty.
people.bio:
Mohamed, founder of the brief Devadu dynasty, reigned from 1692 to 1701. His Arabic title Sultan Al-Ghazi Muhammad II carried the honorific al-Ghazi ("warrior"), suggesting military achievements. His father was Haji Ali Thukkala Ma'afai Kilege, and his mother was Kakuni Dio of Devadhu Island in Huvadu Atoll, from which the dynasty took its name. His grandfather Muhammad al-Kabir Fadiyaru had been based in Addu Atoll.

His rise to power represented a geographic shift in Maldivian politics, bringing to the fore families from the southern atolls rather than the traditional Male-centered elite. He married three times, and remarkably his third wife was once again Maryam Kabafa'anu, who was killed in 1701. Mohamed himself died on January 16, 1701 at Male, ending the Devadu dynasty after a single reign of approximately nine years.

facts: Founder figure for the Isdu dynasty interval.
His line remained relevant through kin links after deposition.
people.no: Isdu Dynasty.
people.bio:
Ali VII, founder of the short-lived Isdu dynasty, came to the throne upon the death of the Devadu sultan in 1701. His full Arabic title, Sultan Ali VII ibnu Vazir Ibrahim Shahbandaru, indicated his father Ibrahim Shahbandaru Kilege held the important office of Shahbandaru (harbor master). He married Aisha Rani Kilege and fathered Hassan X (P75), his immediate successor.

Ali VII's reign lasted barely a year; he died on October 27, 1701. Despite the brevity of his rule, his establishment of the Isdu line created a new dynastic faction that would contest power for several years before being displaced by the Dhiyamigili dynasty in 1704.

people.no: Son of #74. Deposed by cousin.
facts: Secondary chronology describes his reign as very short during the Isdu-Dhiyamigili transition crisis.

known_as.name: Addu Hassan Manikfan
known_as.note: Attributed in secondary chronologies; kept as uncertain pending stronger archival corroboration.

people.bio:
Hassan X, sometimes known by the sobriquet Addu Hassan Manikfan, succeeded his father Ali VII (P74) immediately upon the latter's death on October 27, 1701. His mother was Aisha Rani Kilege. He married Thubbeegedharu Kilavethi Aisha Manikufa'anu and fathered three sons and one daughter.

His reign was cut short when his cousin Ibrahim Mudzhiruddine (P76) seized the throne. Hassan was captured and banished to Hithadu in Addu Atoll on January 1, 1705, beginning a remarkably long exile. He survived in banishment for over half a century, finally dying at Hithadu in June 1758 -- one of the longest-lived exiled monarchs in Maldivian history.

facts: Final ruler of the Isdu dynasty segment before Dhiyamigili takeover.
people.no: Deposed on Haj. Father was brother of #74.
people.bio:
Ibrahim Mudzhiruddine, bearing the Arabic laqab Muzhir al-Din meaning "Manifester of the Faith," was the last sultan of the Isdu dynasty, reigning from 1701 to 1704. His father was Hassan Ma'afai Thakurufa'anu, a brother of Ali VII's (P74) father Ibrahim Shahbandaru, making him a first cousin of Hassan X (P75) whom he had deposed.

His mother was Athiri Kama'ana Khadija Dio. He married Fatima Kabafa'anu and fathered a son, Fua Mulaku Muhammad Manikufa'anu. His reign ended in a manner both inglorious and ironic: while absent from the Maldives on the Hajj pilgrimage to Mecca, his throne was seized on December 20, 1704, by Mohamed Imaduddine II (P77) of the Dhiyamigili family. Ibrahim Mudzhiruddine never returned to power.

facts: Founder sovereign of the Dhiyamigili dynasty period.
Associated with the overthrow of Isdu rule in 1704.
Born 1661 at Dhiyamigili, Kolumadulu Atoll (project had yb:1675).
Father was Ibrahim Dorimena Ma'afai Kilege Thakurufa'anu, a vizier. Mother was Amina Dio.
Grandfather was Muhammad Tohali Thakuru of Dhiyamigili.
Founder of the Dhiyamigili dynasty. Deposed the Isdu line in December 1704.
Four marriages. Children include Ibrahim Iskandar II (P78) and Mohamed Imaduddine III (P79).
Died October 22, 1721 at Dhiyamigili Palace, Male.
people.no: Dhiyamigili Dynasty.

known_as.name: Sultan al-Mu'azzam Muzaffar Muhammad Imad ud-din II al-Afham
known_as.note: Complete Arabic titulature.

known_as.name: Dhiyamigili Bodu Bandaarain
known_as.note: Dynastic epithet from Dhiyamigili island.

people.bio:
Mohamed Imaduddine II, known as Dhiyamigili Bodu Bandaarain, was the founder of the Dhiyamigili dynasty and one of the most politically formidable sultans of the eighteenth century. His Arabic laqab 'Imad al-Din, meaning "Pillar of the Faith," was the second instance of this title in Maldivian sovereignty. Born in 1661 at Dhiyamigili island in Kolumadulu Atoll -- far from the political center of Male -- he was the son of Ibrahim Dorimena Ma'afai Kilege Thakurufa'anu, a vizier, and Amina Dio.

His path to power was that of an outsider who seized opportunity. When Ibrahim Mudzhiruddine (P76) departed on the Hajj in late 1704, Mohamed Imaduddine deposed the Isdu regent and established himself as sultan on December 20, 1704. His full Arabic titulature, Sultan al-Mu'azzam Muzaffar Muhammad Imad ud-din II al-Afham, proclaimed him both "The Magnificent" (al-Mu'azzam) and "The Victorious" (Muzaffar).

He married four times, and his children included two future sultans: Ibrahim Iskandar II (P78) and Mohamed Imaduddine III (P79). His reign of sixteen years, from 1704 until his death on October 22, 1721 at Dhiyamigili Palace in Male, was marked by the consolidation of a new dynastic order. The Dhiyamigili dynasty would rule the Maldives for most of the eighteenth century, until the devastating Malabar invasion of 1752 brought the line to near-extinction.

people.no: Son of #77.
people.bio:
Ibrahim Iskander II, bearing the Hellenistic throne epithet Iskandar ("Alexander"), was the elder son and successor of Mohamed Imaduddine II (P77). Born on September 11, 1708 at Kabafa'anu Palace in Male, his mother was Amina Kabadi Kilege, one of his father's four wives. Named heir presumptive as early as 1715, he succeeded to the throne upon his father's death in 1720.

His thirty-year reign from 1720 to 1750 was the longest of the Dhiyamigili period. He married five times, and his children included Sultan Muhammad Ghiyathuddine (P81), who would become the last Dhiyamigili sultan. He died on February 24, 1750 at Male, leaving the throne to his younger brother Mohamed Imaduddine III (P79), who would face the catastrophic Malabar invasion just two years later.

facts: Penultimate Dhiyamigili sovereign before the 1766 restoration shift.
Abducted December 18, 1752 by Ali Raja of Cannanore; blinded; died June 25, 1757 at Minicoy in exile.
people.no: Son of #77. Born January 14, 1717. Abducted by Ali Raja of Cannanore in 1752, blinded and exiled to Minicoy.
people.bio:
Mohamed Imaduddine III, the third sultan to bear the laqab 'Imad al-Din ("Pillar of the Faith"), suffered one of the most harrowing fates of any Maldivian sovereign. Born on January 14, 1717 at Kabafa'anu Palace in Male, he was the full brother of Ibrahim Iskandar II (P78), sharing the same mother, Amina Kabadi Kilege. He succeeded his brother in February 1750.

His reign was cut brutally short on December 18, 1752, when forces loyal to Ali Raja of Cannanore (the Arakkal Kingdom of Kerala) invaded the Maldives. The sultan was abducted and transported to Kavaratti island in the Laccadives, where he was blinded -- a traditional method of rendering a deposed ruler incapable of regaining power. He languished in exile on Minicoy island until his death on June 25, 1757.

The Malabar seizure of 1752 plunged the Maldives into its most severe crisis since the Portuguese period, as Cannanore forces occupied Male. It was during this interregnum that the Huraagey family, descended from the Portuguese-era Hilaaly exile kings, rose to prominence. Hassan Izzuddine (P80) would eventually expel the Malabar occupiers and establish a new dynasty.

facts: Founder of the Huraagey dynasty (Donbandaarain).
Documented as descending from Joao (#66) through the Dom Luis branch.
Founder of the Huraagey dynasty, descended from the Portuguese-era Hilaaly royal house through Dom Luis de Souza lineage.

Five-generation genealogical bridge: Dom Manoel (P61) → Dom Joao (P66) → Dona Ines (P213) → Dom Luis de Souza (P97) → Dom Maraduru Fandiaiy Kaleygefan (P98) → Hussain Daharada Kaleygefan (P99) → Mohamed Faamuladeyri Thakurufan (P100) → P80.
The Huraagey dynasty he founded ruled the Maldives for approximately 200 years until the abolition of the monarchy in 1968.
people.no: Huraagey Dynasty founder (Donbandaarain). Descended from Hilaaly King Joao (#66) through Dom Luis line.

known_as.note: Popular historical name; "Don" prefix from Portuguese-era Hilaaly lineage, "Bandaarain" from the office/title cluster.

known_as.name: Muleegey Don Hassan Maniku
known_as.note: House-linked historical rendering incorporating Muleege house name.

known_as.name: Don Bandaara
known_as.note: Variant transliteration tied to the same ruler identity.

offices_held.label: De facto regent
offices_held.note: Described as de facto regent before accession.

people.bio:
Hassan Izzuddine, popularly known as Dhon Bandaarain, was the founder of the Huraagey dynasty and one of the most consequential figures in Maldivian history. His Arabic laqab 'Izz al-Din, meaning "Glory of the Faith," was uniquely appropriate for a man who liberated his country from foreign occupation. Born around 1728, he was the son of Mohamed Faamuladeyri Thakurufan (P100) and traced his lineage through five generations back to Dom Joao (P66) of the Portuguese-era Hilaaly house, via the remarkable genealogical bridge through Dona Ines (P213) and Dom Luis de Souza (P97).

The "Don" prefix in his popular name Dhon Bandaarain preserved the memory of his Portuguese-Maldivian ancestry. When the forces of Ali Raja of Cannanore occupied the Maldives in 1752 following the abduction of Sultan Mohamed Imaduddine III (P79), Hassan emerged as the de facto leader of the resistance. He served as regent from approximately 1754, and after expelling the Malabar occupiers, was installed as sultan on Friday, December 7, 1759.

The liberation came at personal cost: Hassan lost his left thumb in battle, a detail preserved in the chronicle traditions. He married Amina Manikufa'anu, known as Bodu Didi, and fathered three sons: Mohamed Muizzuddine (P83), Ibrahim Don Goma, and Ali (who would become Hassan Nooredine I, P84).

Hassan Izzuddine died on February 1, 1767 at Veyodhoshu Palace in Male. The Huraagey dynasty he founded would rule the Maldives for over two centuries, through the British protectorate period, the constitutional reforms of the 1930s, and the independence era, until the monarchy was abolished by referendum in 1968. The dynasty's improbable origin -- tracing through a Portuguese Catholic marriage in seventeenth-century Goa back to a Maldivian sultan who had converted from Islam -- makes it one of the most genealogically complex royal houses in the Indian Ocean world.

facts: Last Dhiyamigili sovereign in the standard succession lists.
Born before October 6, 1733 at Kabafa'anu Palace, Male.
Drowned off Guraidu island October 8, 1774.
Secondary sources credit him with the first known Thaana dictionary compilation.
He is remembered in some narratives as a martyr ruler (Shaheed Kulha Bandaarain).
people.no: Son of #78. Born before Oct 6, 1733 (RoyalArk). Drowned off Guraidu.

known_as.name: Muhammadh Manikfaan
known_as.note: Name-form recorded in historical summaries.

known_as.name: Haajee Bandaarain
known_as.note: Historical epithet linked to Sultan Muhammad Ghiyath al-Din.

offices_held.label: Sultan
offices_held.note: Final Dhiyamigili sovereign in standard succession lists.

people.bio:
Mohamed Ghiyathuddine, bearing the Arabic laqab Ghiyath al-Din meaning "Succor of the Faith," was the last sultan of the Dhiyamigili dynasty and a figure remembered with both reverence and pathos. Born before October 6, 1733 at Kabafa'anu Palace in Male, he was the son of Ibrahim Iskandar II (P78) and Aisha Kabafa'anu. His popular epithet Haajee Bandaarain reflected his completion of the Hajj pilgrimage.

He came to the throne in 1766 as a Dhiyamigili restoration figure after the Huraagey founder Hassan Izzuddine (P80) died, representing an attempt to return the older dynasty to power. He married three times and fathered a son, Abdullah Ibrahim, born in 1760. Secondary sources credit him with compiling the first known Thaana dictionary, making him a figure of cultural as well as political significance.

His reign ended tragically when he drowned off Guraidu island on October 8, 1774. Some historical narratives remember him as Shaheed Kulha Bandaarain, a martyr ruler, suggesting that his death may not have been entirely accidental. His drowning definitively ended the Dhiyamigili dynasty and restored the Huraagey line to permanent power.

facts: Transitional Huraagey ruler during the post-Dhiyamigili settlement.
Abdicated in favor of his cousin.
people.no: Abdicated for cousin.
people.bio:
Mohamed Shamsuddine II, bearing the laqab Shams al-Din ("Sun of the Faith"), held the throne for a mere five months in 1774. His birth name was Muhammad Manikufa'anu, and his father was Husain Bodu Dorimena Kilegefa'anu from the Huraagey family. He seized the throne on December 22, 1773, following the drowning of the Dhiyamigili sultan Mohamed Ghiyathuddine (P81).

His brief reign ended in voluntary abdication in May 1774, when he stepped aside in favor of his cousin Mohamed Muizzuddine (P83), the son of the Huraagey founder Hassan Izzuddine (P80). He died on March 31, 1779 at Male. Despite the brevity of his rule, his seizure of the throne confirmed that the post-Dhiyamigili succession would remain within the Huraagey family.

people.no: Son of #80.

known_as.name: Kalhu Bandaarain
known_as.note: Popularly known-by name in royal chronologies.

offices_held.label: Sultan
offices_held.note: Early Huraagey reign after Dhiyamigili restoration shift.

people.bio:
Mohamed Muizzuddine, popularly known as Kalhu Bandaarain, was the eldest son of the Huraagey founder Hassan Izzuddine (P80) and the sultan who consolidated his father's new dynasty. His Arabic laqab Mu'izz al-Din, meaning "Fortifier of the Faith," reflected the strengthening role he played. Born before October 28, 1753, his mother was Amina Bodu Didi, the wife of P80.

He assumed the throne in May 1774 following the abdication of his cousin Mohamed Shamsuddine II (P82). He married twice -- first Amina Manikufa'anu in Addu Atoll in 1773, then Donbina'age Amina Didi in Male in 1774 -- and fathered a daughter, Princess Kaka'age Kalu Goma Aisha Kaba'a Rani Kilege, born in 1776.

His reign of five years from 1774 to 1779 consolidated Huraagey control of the succession. He died on September 13, 1779 at Male, and was succeeded by his younger brother Hassan Nooredine I (P84).

facts: Early consolidator of the Huraagey line after its restoration period.
Birth name was Prince Ali. Born before July 13, 1763.
Mother was Amina Manikufa'anu (Bodu Didi), wife of P80.
Three sons: Prince Husain Ali (died on Mecca pilgrimage), Prince Abdul-Hamid, and Sultan Muhammad Mu'in ud-din I (P85).
Died June 2, 1799 at Jeddah from smallpox during Hajj pilgrimage — one of at least two Maldivian sultans who died abroad on pilgrimage.
people.no: Younger brother of #83.

known_as.name: Sultan Shah Haji al-Hassan Nur ud-din Iskander
known_as.note: Complete title with Shah and Hajji honorifics.

people.bio:
Hassan Nooredine I, bearing the laqab Nur al-Din meaning "Light of the Faith," was the younger son of the Huraagey founder Hassan Izzuddine (P80) and the brother of Mohamed Muizzuddine (P83). His birth name was Prince Ali, and he was born before July 13, 1763. His mother, like his brother's, was Amina Manikufa'anu (Bodu Didi).

His twenty-year reign from 1779 to 1799 was a period of relative stability for the young Huraagey dynasty. He married twice and fathered three sons: Prince Husain Ali, who died on a Mecca pilgrimage; Prince Abdul-Hamid; and Sultan Muhammad Mu'in ud-din I (P85), who would succeed him.

Hassan Nooredine I met his end far from the Maldives. He died on June 2, 1799 at Jeddah from smallpox contracted during the Hajj pilgrimage, one of at least two Maldivian sultans to die abroad on pilgrimage. His death while performing a sacred religious duty lent his memory a pious distinction.

people.no: Son of #84.
people.bio:
Mohamed Mueenuddine I, bearing the laqab Mu'in al-Din meaning "Helper of the Faith," was the son and successor of Hassan Nooredine I (P84). His mother was Amina, the second wife of P84. He was appointed regent on January 24, 1799, when his father departed for the Hajj, and became sultan upon receiving news of his father's death at Jeddah on June 2, 1799.

His reign of thirty-six years from 1799 to 1835 was the longest of the early Huraagey period. He married twice: first Amina Manikufa'anu, who died around 1829, and then Khadija Didi. His son by the second marriage, Sultan Muhammad Imad ud-din IV (P86), would succeed him and reign for an even longer period. He died on January 21, 1835 at Male.

people.no: Son of #85.
people.bio:
Mohamed Imaduddine IV, the fourth sultan to bear the laqab 'Imad al-Din ("Pillar of the Faith"), reigned for an extraordinary forty-seven years from 1835 to 1882, the longest reign of any Huraagey sultan. Born in 1818, he was the son of Mohamed Mueenuddine I (P85) and his second wife Khadija Didi.

His first wife, Kudaeduruge Zulaikha Rani Kilege, was notable as a commoner whom the sultan elevated to noble rank, a gesture that demonstrated both royal prerogative and personal attachment. His second wife was Maryam Didi. Among his children were Sultan Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87) and a blind son, Hassan Izz ud-din (P222), who was excluded from the succession on account of his disability but whose sons -- Mohamed Mueenuddine II (P88) and Mohamed Imaduddine VI (P92) -- both became sultans.

Mohamed Imaduddine IV's long reign encompassed the period when the Maldives first entered into formal relations with the British Empire, ultimately becoming a British protectorate in 1887. He died on January 10, 1882 at Male, five years before the protectorate agreement was signed.

people.no: Son of #86. Two accessions.
facts: Mother was Kudaeduruge Zulaikha Rani Kilege, a commoner elevated to noble rank by P86.
Five wives: Don Didi, Kakage Don Goma (mother of P91), Bodugaluge Don Didi (mother of P90), Ekgamuge Medu Didi, Henveyruge Don Didi.
Died November 29, 1892 at Male.

known_as.name: Sultan Ibrahim Nur ud-din II
known_as.note: Arabic laqab form.

people.bio:
Ibrahim Nooredine II, bearing the laqab Nur al-Din ("Light of the Faith"), was one of the most domestically complex sultans in Maldivian history. The son of Mohamed Imaduddine IV (P86) and his commoner wife Zulaikha Rani Kilege, he held the throne across two separate accessions: first from 1882 to 1886, and again from 1888 to 1892.

His personal life was marked by five marriages: to Don Didi, Kakage Don Goma, Bodugaluge Don Didi, Ekgamuge Medu Didi, and Henveyruge Don Didi. Through Kakage Don Goma he fathered Mohamed Shamsuddine III (P91), who would proclaim the first Maldivian constitution. Through Bodugaluge Don Didi he fathered Mohamed Imaduddine V (P90), who would reign briefly as an infant sultan.

Between his two accessions, his nephew Mohamed Mueenuddine II (P88) briefly held the throne from 1886 to 1888. Ibrahim Nooredine's return to power for his second accession demonstrated the dynastic resilience of his branch of the Huraagey family. He died on November 29, 1892 at Male, setting off a succession crisis that would see three different sultans within a single year.

Ibrahim Nooredine's broader significance lies in his position as the grandfather, through his daughter, of the last sultan Mohamed Farid (P95), making him the patriarchal ancestor of the final generation of Maldivian royalty.

people.no: Nephew of #87; grandson of #86.
people.bio:
Mohamed Mueenuddine II, bearing the laqab Mu'in al-Din ("Helper of the Faith"), reigned from 1886 to 1888 between the two accessions of his uncle Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87). His father was not P86 or P87 directly, but rather Hassan Izz ud-din (P222), the blind son of Mohamed Imaduddine IV (P86) who had been excluded from the succession on account of his disability.

His mother was Handeygirige Kahambuge Aisha Bodu Didi. He married twice and fathered several children, most notably Prince Ali Majehi Ganduvaru Manippulu, who would later reign as Sultan Hassan Nooredine II (P94). He abdicated on October 27, 1885, yielding the throne back to his uncle P87.

Mohamed Mueenuddine II died on September 23, 1892 at Male. His branch of the family, descending from the blind prince Hassan Izz ud-din, would continue to contest the succession with the descendants of Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87), a dynastic rivalry that shaped Maldivian politics through the early twentieth century.

people.no: Infant. Son of #87.
people.bio:
Mohamed Imaduddine V, the fifth sultan to bear the laqab 'Imad al-Din ("Pillar of the Faith"), was one of the youngest and shortest-reigning sultans in Maldivian history. Born in 1884 at Male, he was the son of Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87) and his wife Bodugaluge Don Didi. He was placed on the throne as an infant following the rapid succession of deaths in 1892, but was deposed after only three months on May 20, 1893.

He married Fatima Didi and had two sons, neither of whom became sultan. After his deposition, he lived in relative obscurity. He died in June 1920 at Bombay while en route to the Mecca pilgrimage, far from the throne he had briefly occupied as a baby.

facts: Long-serving late-monarchy ruler across two accessions.
Represents the mature constitutional phase before the final sultans.
Born November 1879 at Male (RoyalArk). CONTRADICTION: earlier project data had yb:1863 — possibly confused with P92 who was born ca. 1863.
Proclaimed first Maldivian Constitution December 22, 1932.
Mother was Kakage Don Goma.
people.no: Son of #87. Two accessions. Born Nov 1879 (RoyalArk; earlier data had 1863, likely confusion with P92).
people.bio:
Mohamed Shamsuddine III, bearing the laqab Shams al-Din meaning "Sun of the Faith," was the sultan who ushered the Maldives into the modern constitutional era. Born in November 1879 at Male, he was the son of Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87) and Kakage Don Goma. His full regnal titulature, Sultan Muhammad Shams ud-din III Iskander, appended the Hellenistic throne epithet Iskander ("Alexander") to his Arabic laqab.

His path to the throne was circuitous. First placed on the throne on May 18, 1893, he was deposed after barely two months on July 21, 1893, replaced by his half-uncle Mohamed Imaduddine VI (P92). He regained the throne on March 12, 1903, when P92 was dethroned, and thereafter held power for three decades.

His greatest achievement was the proclamation of the first Maldivian Constitution on December 22, 1932, a landmark document that transformed the absolute sultanate into a constitutional monarchy. The constitution represented the culmination of reformist pressures that had been building throughout the British protectorate period. He married three times -- Maveyodoruge Sanfa'a Didi (divorced), Kalu-Hura'age Sitti Didi, and Medu Ganduvaru Dori Goma -- and fathered one son and three daughters.

His constitutional experiment proved double-edged: he was himself deposed for unconstitutional conduct on October 2, 1934. He died on March 12, 1935 at Moonimaa'ge, Male, just months after losing power. Despite his downfall, the constitutional principle he established proved irreversible, and subsequent Maldivian governance would never fully return to absolute rule.

people.no: Grandson of #86. Deposed in Suez.
people.bio:
Mohamed Imaduddine VI, the sixth and final sultan to bear the laqab 'Imad al-Din ("Pillar of the Faith"), reigned from 1892 to 1903. Born around 1863 as Prince Manduge Hassan Nur ud-din, he was the son of the blind prince Hassan Izz ud-din (P222) and Nabila Manduge Don Didi. He shared the same father as Mohamed Mueenuddine II (P88), making them half-brothers.

Recognized as sultan by the British on August 3, 1893, his decade-long reign coincided with the consolidation of the British protectorate over the Maldives. He married four times and fathered six children. His dethroning on March 12, 1903 brought Mohamed Shamsuddine III (P91) back to the throne.

After his deposition, Mohamed Imaduddine VI went into exile, eventually settling in Egypt where he lived for nearly three decades. He married his fourth wife, Sharifa Khanum, at Suez in December 1902. He died in Egypt on September 30, 1932, never having returned to the Maldives -- a distant echo of Dom Manoel's exile centuries earlier.

facts: Born April 21, 1887 at Male.
Invested February 23, 1935; crowned July 21, 1938.
Forced abdication April 8, 1943.
people.no: Son of #88. Born April 21, 1887. Died April 15, 1967.
people.bio:
Hassan Nooredine II, bearing the laqab Nur al-Din meaning "Light of the Faith," was the penultimate sultan of the Maldives. Born on April 21, 1887 at Male as Prince Ali Majehi Ganduvaru Manippulu, he was the son of Mohamed Mueenuddine II (P88) and Zulaikha Rani Kilege.

His accession came in the wake of Mohamed Shamsuddine III's (P91) constitutional crisis. He was invested on February 23, 1935, and formally crowned on July 21, 1938. He married four times and fathered seven children, including three sons and four daughters. His son Muhammad Nur ud-din would later become Head of the Royal House after the monarchy's abolition.

His forced abdication on April 8, 1943, ended his reign after eight years. The deposition reflected the growing power of elected politicians and reformists who saw the sultanate as an obstacle to modernization. He lived for nearly a quarter century after losing the throne, dying on April 15, 1967 at Ehgamuge, Male, just one year before the monarchy he had embodied was formally abolished.

facts: Last ruling Sultan before the monarchy ended.
Maternal line links back to Ibrahim Nooredine (#87).
people.no: Last sultan. Mother Don Goma was daughter of Ibrahim Nooredine (#87).

offices_held.label: Sultan
offices_held.note: Final ruling Sultan before abolition of the monarchy.

people.bio:
Mohamed Farid, the last sultan of the Maldives, presided over the transformation of a centuries-old monarchy into a modern republic. Born in 1901 at Male, he was the son of Amir Amin Abdul-Majid Didi (P111), whose own connection to the royal house ran through the Athireegey (Huraagey collateral) family. His mother, Princess Veyoge Don Goma (also known as Ruqia Don Goma), was a daughter of Sultan Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87), providing the maternal royal link that legitimized his claim.

Chosen as sultan on January 22, 1954 and formally proclaimed on March 6, 1954, his regnal title Jalal ul-Malik Muhammad Farid al-Awwal Kathiri Maha Radun combined Arabic honorifics with the ancient Dhivehi royal designation. When the British protectorate ended on July 26, 1965, he assumed the grander title of "King" (His Majesty King Muhammad Farid the First), a short-lived elevation that lasted barely three years.

He married three times. The sultanate was abolished by national referendum on April 1, 1968, and the Republic of the Maldives was formally declared on November 11, 1968, ending a royal tradition that stretched back over eight centuries to the legendary Koimala. Mohamed Farid died in Male in May 1969, less than a year after the institution he had embodied ceased to exist.

With his passing, the Huraagey dynasty and the Maldivian sultanate became historical artifacts. The dynasty's extraordinary genealogical journey -- from the Hilaaly warlords of the fifteenth century, through a Catholic conversion in Portuguese Cochin, across five generations of Luso-Maldivian descent in Goa, to a liberation hero who expelled Malabar invaders, and finally to a constitutional monarch overtaken by republicanism -- encapsulates the full sweep of Maldivian political history.

people.no: Brother of Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104). Co-liberator of the Maldives from Portuguese rule in 1573.
people.bio: Ali Thakurufaanu was one of the three brothers from Utheemu island who liberated the Maldives from Portuguese occupation in 1573. Together with his brothers Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104), the national hero, and Hassan Thakurufaanu (P201), Ali conducted the guerrilla campaign that overthrew the Portuguese-installed regent Andiri Andirin. Ali's role in the liberation is commemorated alongside his brothers in Maldivian national memory.

people.no: Brother of Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104). Co-liberator of the Maldives from Portuguese rule in 1573.
people.bio: Hassan Thakurufaanu was the third of the Utheemu brothers who liberated the Maldives from Portuguese rule in 1573. Alongside his brothers Mohamed Thakurufaanu (P104) and Ali Thakurufaanu (P200), Hassan participated in the armed resistance campaign that ended fifteen years of Portuguese domination. The three brothers are jointly honored in Maldivian historical tradition as the architects of national liberation.

people.no: Daughter of Aboobakuru (#40). Mother of Sheikh Hassan (#49).
people.bio: Kamba Dio was a daughter of Abu Bakar I (P40), a son of the Hilaaly dynasty founder Hassan al-Hilali. She served as a critical matrilineal link in the Hilaaly succession: her son Sheikh Hassan (P49) became the forty-ninth sovereign through his descent from her. She married Talamedi Kilege. Kamba Dio's role illustrates the continuing importance of royal women as conduits of dynastic legitimacy in the Maldivian system.

people.no: Mother of Hali (#11) and Keimi (#12).
people.bio: Aidage Maava Kilege was an early Lunar dynasty matriarch who was the mother of two sultans: Hali (P11) and Keimi (P12). She was married to Vengihi Kalo (P204), the father of Hali, though Keimi's father was a different man. Her role in the succession exemplifies the matrilineal principle that characterized early Maldivian kingship, where the mother's royal status determined legitimacy regardless of the father's identity.

people.no: Father of Hali (#11). Husband of Aidage Maava Kilege (P203).
people.bio: Vengihi Kalo was the father of Hali (P11), the eleventh sovereign, and the husband of Aidage Maava Kilege (P203). He was not himself a sovereign, but his marriage to a woman of royal matrilineal standing produced a son who held the throne. The identification of Vengihi Kalo in the king-lists is unusual for this period, when fathers were often unnamed while mothers were consistently recorded.

people.no: Parent of Hudhei (#9).
people.bio: Hiriya Maava Kilege was the mother of Hudhei (P9), the ninth sovereign. She was a different woman from Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage (P216), who mothered the preceding four sovereigns (P5 through P8). The transition of the throne from the sons of Fahi Hiriya to the son of Hiriya Maava Kilege marked a shift between two matrilineal branches of the Lunar dynasty in the mid-thirteenth century.

people.no: Father of Hassan (#34) and Isa (#35).
people.bio: Maayin Rannabandeyri Kilege was the father of two sultans: Hassan II (P34) and Isa (P35). His name incorporates the Rannabandeyri title, one of the traditional Maldivian court offices, indicating his position within the Hilaaly-era aristocratic hierarchy. Both his sons held the throne briefly during the interregnum of 1410-1411 before being displaced by the restoration of Ibrahim I (P31).

people.no: Daughter of Aboobakuru (#40). Mother of Ali (#57).
people.bio: Recca was a daughter of Abu Bakar I (P40), a son of the Hilaaly dynasty founder. Through her marriage to Al-Amir Muhammad Farina Kilege Kalo Mavadi Koya, she became the mother of Ali V (P57), the fifty-seventh sovereign. The RoyalArk sources give her name as Princess Rekka. Her son Ali was assassinated by his own sister Buraki Rani in 1514, an event that illustrates the deadly internal rivalries within the extended Hilaaly family.

people.no: Daughter of Kalu Mohamed (#51). Wife of Ali (#63).
people.bio: Princess Aysha Rani Kilege was a daughter of Kalu Mohamed (P51) and the wife of Ali V (P57). Her marriage united two branches of the Hilaaly royal house: she was the daughter of one of the most persistent claimants to the throne, while her husband traced his maternal descent from Abu Bakar I (P40). Despite this marital alliance, Aysha's husband was assassinated in 1514, demonstrating that kinship ties could not always restrain political violence in the late Hilaaly period.

people.no: Son of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. Sailed to Europe 1561; killed in street brawl at Lisbon after Feb 1583.
people.bio: Dom Francisco de Malvidas was the eldest son of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide, born in Portuguese India. In 1561 he sailed to Europe, one of the few members of the Maldivian royal family to set foot on the Iberian Peninsula. His life ended in violence when he was killed in a street brawl in Lisbon sometime after February 1583, a fate that speaks to the precarious existence of displaced colonial aristocrats far from their titular kingdoms.

people.no: Son of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. Removed to Goa 1591; returned Cochin 1606; died after 1610.
people.bio: Dom Pedro de Malvidas was a son of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. He was removed to Goa in 1591, returned to Cochin in 1606, and married a Portuguese-Indian noblewoman. He died after 1610. Like his siblings, he lived entirely within the Portuguese colonial world, never seeing the Maldivian islands his father had once ruled.

people.no: Daughter of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. Born before Jan 27, 1555 at Goa.
people.bio: Dona Leonor de Malvidas, born before January 27, 1555 at Goa, was a daughter of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. Named after her mother, she was raised in the Portuguese colonial society of Goa as a Catholic noblewoman of Maldivian royal descent.

people.no: Daughter of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide.
people.bio: Dona Catarina de Malvidas was a daughter of Dom Manoel (P61) and Dona Leonor de Ataide. Details of her life are sparse in the surviving records, but her existence is documented in the RoyalArk genealogical reconstruction of the Portuguese-era Hilaaly family.

people.no: Daughter of Dom Joao (P66) and Dona Francisca de Vasconcelos (P101). Married Captain Sebastiao Tavares de Souza at Goa 1610. Mother of Dom Luis de Souza (P97). Critical link in the Hilaaly-to-Huraagey bridge.
people.bio:
Infanta Dona Ines de Malvidas occupies one of the most genealogically significant positions in Maldivian royal history, though she herself never set foot in the Maldives. The daughter of Dom Joao (P66) and Dona Francisca de Vasconcelos (P101), she was the sister of Dom Philippe (P67), the last male-line Hilaaly king.

In 1610 at Goa she married Captain Sebastiao Tavares de Souza, a Portuguese fidalgo who served as Captain of Canara Fort (1610-1612) and later of Daman (1612). Through this marriage she became the mother of Dom Luis de Souza (P97), the titular King of the Maldives from approximately 1639 to 1656.

Dona Ines is the critical bridge figure in the Hilaaly-to-Huraagey genealogical transition. When her brother Dom Philippe died without issue before 1639, the royal bloodline could only continue through her descendants. The five-generation chain from Dona Ines through Dom Luis, Dom Maraduru (P98), Hussain Daharada (P99), Mohamed Faamuladeyri (P100), to Hassan Izzuddine (P80) represents one of the most remarkable genealogical bridges in Indian Ocean history, connecting a Catholic Portuguese marriage in seventeenth-century Goa to the Muslim dynasty that ruled the Maldives until 1968.

people.no: Mother of four sultans: Dhinei (#5), Dhilhel (#6), Wadi (#7), Valla Dio (#8). Important early Lunar matriarch.
people.bio: Fahi Hiriya Ma'ava'a Kilage was one of the most important early Lunar dynasty matriarchs. She was the mother of four consecutive sultans: Dhinei (P5), Dhilhel (P6), Wadi (P7), and Valla Dio (P8). Her four sons reigned in unbroken fraternal succession from approximately 1193 to 1258, a span of over sixty years. Through Wadi's son Audha (P13), she was the ancestress of the entire later Lunar dynasty, including Omar Veeru (P18), Khadijah (P20), and Raadhaafathi (P25). No other individual in the early Maldivian succession produced as many direct royal heirs.

people.no: Father of Hassan al-Hilali (#30) and Hussain (#32). Also known as Kuliveri Hilalu Kaivulanna Kaloge of Hulule, North Male Atoll. Son of Abbas al-Hilal from Malabar.
people.bio: Dori Kuja, also known as Kuliveri Hilalu Kaivulanna Kaloge of Hulule in North Male Atoll, was the father of the Hilaaly dynasty's twin founders: Hassan al-Hilali (P30) and Hussain (P32). His own father was Abbas al-Hilal, a man of Malabar origin in southern India, from whom the Hilaaly dynasty took its name. Dori Kuja's Malabar connection places the dynasty's origins within the broader network of Indian Ocean Muslim merchant-scholar families who had been active in the Maldives since the twelfth-century conversion.

people.no: Spouse of Hassan al-Hilali (#30).
people.bio: Golavehi Kabulo was the wife of Hassan al-Hilali (P30), the founder of the Hilaaly dynasty. Together they produced a remarkably prolific royal line, with four of their sons becoming sultans: Ibrahim I (P31), Yoosuf II (P39), Abu Bakar I (P40), and possibly a fourth. Through these sons, Golavehi Kabulo was the ancestress of virtually the entire later Hilaaly dynasty, making her one of the most genealogically significant women in Maldivian royal history.

people.no: Son of Kalu Mohamed (#51). Father of Mohamed (#60). Intermediate link between P51 and P60.
people.bio: Umar Ma'afai Kilege was a son of Kalu Mohamed (P51), the thrice-reigning Hilaaly sultan. He was the father of Mohamed (P60), the sixtieth sovereign. His wife was Golavehi Aisha Rani Kilege (P221), who after Umar's death or divorce married his brother Ahmad Manikufa'anu (P220) and bore Hassan IX / Dom Manoel (P61). Umar thus served as a critical genealogical link between Kalu Mohamed and the final generation of Hilaaly sultans.

people.no: Son of Kalu Mohamed (#51). Father of Hassan IX / Dom Manoel (#61). Intermediate link between P51 and P61.
people.bio: Ahmad Manikufa'anu Kalaminja was a son of Kalu Mohamed (P51) and the father of Hassan IX, later known as Dom Manoel (P61). He married Golavehi Aisha Rani Kilege (P221), the former wife of his brother Umar Ma'afai Kilege (P219). Through his son Dom Manoel, Ahmad's line became the ancestor of the Christian-converted Hilaaly branch that maintained titular sovereignty from Goa, and ultimately of the Huraagey dynasty that ruled the Maldives until 1968.

people.no: Mother of both Mohamed (#60) and Hassan IX / Dom Manoel (#61). Wife of both Umar Ma'afai Kilege (P219) and Ahmad Manikufa'anu (P220).
people.bio: Golavehi Aisha Rani Kilege was the mother of both Mohamed (P60) and Hassan IX / Dom Manoel (P61), the last two Hilaaly sultans to reign from Male. She married twice: first to Umar Ma'afai Kilege (P219), by whom she had Mohamed, and second to Ahmad Manikufa'anu Kalaminja (P220), by whom she had Hassan IX. Both her husbands were sons of Kalu Mohamed (P51). Her two sons, though sharing a mother, had different fathers, making them maternal half-brothers. Mohamed was assassinated by Hassan in the act of usurpation that inaugurated the Portuguese period of Maldivian history.

people.no: Blind son of Mohamed Imaduddine (#86). Excluded from succession due to blindness. Father of Mohamed Mueenuddine (#88) and Mohamed Imaduddine VI (#92).
people.bio:
Hassan Izz ud-din was the blind son of Sultan Mohamed Imaduddine IV (P86). His disability excluded him from the succession according to Maldivian custom, which required physical soundness in the sovereign. Despite never reigning himself, he became one of the most genealogically consequential figures of the late Huraagey period: both of his sons, Mohamed Mueenuddine II (P88) and Mohamed Imaduddine VI (P92), became sultans, ruling the Maldives during the critical transition into the British protectorate era.

His wife Handeygirige Kahambuge Aisha Bodu Didi bore P88, while Nabila Manduge Don Didi bore P92. The fact that the succession repeatedly passed through the line of a man excluded from kingship on grounds of blindness -- bypassing the more direct line of his brother Ibrahim Nooredine II (P87) -- created a persistent dynastic tension that shaped Maldivian court politics throughout the late nineteenth century.

people.no: Sister of Koimala (#1). Mother of Dhovemi (#2). Key matrilineal link between first two sovereigns.
people.bio: Henevi Ma'ava'a Kilege was the sister of Koimala (P1), the legendary first sovereign, and the mother of Dhovemi (P2), the second sovereign. She represents the key matrilineal link between the first two rulers of the Maldivian succession. Her son Dhovemi's accession through the maternal line, rather than through direct patrilineal descent from Koimala, established the pattern of matrilineal succession that would characterize the early Lunar dynasty.

people.no: Mother of Aima (#10).
people.bio: Hirati Kabadi Kilage was the mother of Aima (P10), the tenth sovereign. She appears in the RoyalArk genealogies alongside her husband Volumidi Bodu Kilege as one of the few early Lunar-era couples for whom both parents of a sovereign are identified. Her son's brief two-year reign and his father's connection to Fehendu suggest a minor branch of the Lunar dynasty that held power briefly in the mid-thirteenth century.

facts: Bridge figure linking Dom Joao line to later Huraa ancestry.
people.no: Daughter of Dom Joao (#66); key bridge into the Huraa line.

facts: Key intermediate ancestor in the Joao-to-Hassan Izzuddine chain.
people.no: Son of Donna Ines; intermediate link between Christian Hilaaly and Huraa successors.
people.bio:
Dom Luis de Souza, born in 1612 at Goa, was the titular King of the Maldive Islands from approximately 1639 to 1656 and the critical genealogical link between the Portuguese-era Hilaaly exile monarchs and the Huraagey dynasty. He was the son of Captain Sebastiao Tavares de Souza, a Portuguese fidalgo who had served as Captain of Canara Fort, and Infanta Dona Ines de Malvidas (P213), the daughter of Dom Joao (P66).

Dom Luis succeeded his maternal uncle Dom Philippe (P67), who died without issue, as titular king around 1639. He visited Portugal in 1641 and was received by King Joao IV, who confirmed his title of Highness. In 1650 he joined a Portuguese expedition to the Maldives and was wounded during an attack on Male. His involvement in a 1653 attempted overthrow of the Portuguese Viceroy led to his arrest and imprisonment. He died unmarried in 1656 aboard a vessel off the Mozambique coast. Despite leaving no legitimate heir, his descendants through possible natural issue eventually produced the Huraagey line.

facts: Recorded in bridge genealogy between Christian Hilaalys and Huraagey founders.
people.no: Intermediate ancestor in the Joao-to-Hassan Izzuddine bridge.
people.bio: Dom Maraduru Fandiaiy Kaleygefan was a descendant of Dom Luis de Souza (P97) who settled in Huraa, Male Atoll, marking the crucial transition of the Portuguese-Maldivian royal bloodline from the colonial diaspora back to the Maldivian islands. His precise dates are uncertain, but he represented the generation that reconnected the Goa-based exile lineage with Maldivian soil, establishing the family at Huraa island that would produce the future Huraagey dynasty.

facts: Named as intermediate paternal ancestor of Hassan Izzuddine (#80).
people.no: Intermediate ancestor in the Hilaaly-Huraagey bridge chain.
people.bio: Hussain Daharada Kaleygefan was the son of Dom Maraduru Fandiaiy Kaleygefan (P98) and an intermediate figure in the genealogical chain connecting the Portuguese-era Hilaaly exiles to the Huraagey dynasty. Living in the Maldives, his family preserved the memory of their royal Portuguese-Maldivian heritage while reintegrating into the island's Islamic society.

facts: Father of Sultan Hassan Izzuddine (#80).
people.no: Father of Hassan Izzuddine (#80) in the Joao bridge lineage.
people.bio: Mohamed Faamuladeyri Thakurufan was the father of Hassan Izzuddine (P80), the founder of the Huraagey dynasty, and thus the immediate progenitor of the royal house that ruled the Maldives from 1759 to 1968. He married Amina Dio and was the son of Hussain Daharada Kaleygefan (P99), placing him as the penultimate link in the five-generation genealogical bridge stretching from Dom Joao (P66) in Portuguese Goa to the Maldivian liberation sultan.

people.no: Consort associated with Dom Joao; mother link for Donna Ines in bridge sources.
people.bio: Dona Francisca de Vasconcelos was the wife of Dom Joao (P66), whom she married at Cochin before November 23, 1587. She was the sister of Captain Antonio Teixeira de Macedo and daughter of Captain Jeronymo Teixeira de Macedo, a Portuguese military officer. She bore Dom Joao two children: Dom Philippe (P67) and Infanta Dona Ines (P213). She survived her husband by many years, dying after 1639 at Goa.

Often styled as Kateeb Mohamed Thakurufan.
National hero of the Maldives who liberated the islands from Portuguese rule in 1573.

known_as.name: Bodu Thakurufaanu
known_as.note: Popular epithet meaning "the Great Thakurufaanu"; universally used in Maldivian historical memory.

known_as.name: Ghazi Mohamed Thakurufaanu al-Auzam
known_as.note: Full formal honorific style combining Arabic laqab (Ghazi = warrior for the faith) and Dhivehi title.

people.bio:
Mohamed Thakurufaanu al-Auzam, universally known as Bodu Thakurufaanu ("the Great Thakurufaanu"), is the preeminent national hero of the Maldives. Born on Utheemu island in Haa Alif Atoll, he was the son of Katheebu Hussain of Utheemu and Lady Aiminaa Dhiye of Ihavandhoo. His family claimed descent from the Hilaaly dynasty through Kalu Hassan Kaleyge of Baararh, Thiladhummathi Atoll.

Together with his brothers Ali Thakurufaanu (P200) and Hassan Thakurufaanu (P201), Mohamed launched a guerrilla campaign against the Portuguese-backed regent Andiri Andirin who governed the Maldives on behalf of Dom Manoel (P61). The brothers built a fast vessel called the Kalhuoffummi on Minicoy Island and conducted daring raids across the atolls, rallying popular support against the Christian administration.

The liberation campaign culminated in 1573 with the overthrow of the regent and the restoration of Islamic governance. Mohamed Thakurufaanu was acclaimed as sultan, establishing the Utheemu dynasty. His full formal style, Ghazi Mohamed Thakurufaanu al-Auzam, combined the Arabic honorific Ghazi ("warrior for the faith") with the superlative al-Auzam ("the Greatest").

The Maldivian National Day (Rabee-al-Awwal) commemorates his victory. His legacy extends beyond military liberation to the cultural reassertion of Islamic identity after a period of Christian governance. The French traveler Pyrard de Laval, who visited the Maldives in the early seventeenth century, referred to the liberation hero as "Bode ta Courou" (Bodu Thakuru, meaning "great lord"), confirming his fame within living memory of the events.

people.bio: Mohamed Amin Didi served as the first President of the Maldives during the brief First Republic of 1953. His presidency marked the first formal break with monarchical governance in Maldivian history, though the republic lasted less than a year before the sultanate was restored. His role in establishing National Day to commemorate Mohamed Thakurufaanu's liberation victory in the late 1940s reflected his commitment to forging a national identity rooted in the anti-colonial narrative.

people.bio: Abdul-Majid Didi served as Sultan-elect from 1944 to 1952, though he never formally took the throne in person. He was the father of Mohamed Farid (P95), the last sultan, and his own father Haji Ibrahim Didi Effendi had been Grand Vizier. His role as a transitional figure between the old sultanate and the final phase of monarchy reflects the complex political negotiations of the late protectorate era.

people.bio: Ibrahim Nasir Rannabandeyri Kilegefan served as the second President of the Maldives from 1968 to 1978, having previously served as Prime Minister. He was the signatory of the independence agreement with Britain on July 26, 1965, representing the King of the Maldives in negotiations with Sir Michael Walker. His documented lineage traces to the Dhiyamigili Sultan Muhammad Ghiyathuddine (P81), connecting him to the royal house through a collateral line. His court-office title Rannabandeyri Kilegefan preserves one of the traditional Maldivian aristocratic designations.

people.bio: Maumoon Abdul Gayoom served as the third President of the Maldives from November 11, 1978 to November 11, 2008, a period of thirty years that made him the longest-serving head of state in Maldivian republican history. His presidency encompassed the transformation of the Maldives into a tourism-based economy and the country's emergence onto the international stage.

people.bio: Mohamed Nasheed served as the fourth President of the Maldives from November 11, 2008 to 2012. Born on May 17, 1967 in Male, he was educated in Male and Colombo before pursuing further studies in Liverpool, Brighton, and Oxford. He published a historical work on the traditional Dhivehi polity of the period 1800-1900 under the title "Dhe Radhun," demonstrating a scholarly engagement with the sultanate history his presidency succeeded.

offices_held.note: Title-bearing elite associated with Handeygiri office lineage naming.

people.bio: Yusuf Handeygirin was a title-bearing member of the Handeygiri office lineage. His son Danna Mohamed (P38) briefly held the sultanate in 1421, making Yusuf a paternal link to the Hilaaly royal succession. The Handeygiri office tradition combined elite status with administrative responsibilities in the Maldivian court.
